## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]
**Define specific soil resistance in tillage. State its typical range for (a) sandy loam and (b) compacted clay.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> **Specific soil resistance** is defined as the horizontal draft force per unit cross-sectional area of soil cut. Mathematically, $K = D / (w \cdot d)$, where $D$ is the draft force (kN), $w$ is the width of cut (m), and $d$ is the depth of cut (m). It is measured in **kPa**.

Typical values:
- **(a) Sandy loam:** $K = 40$ to $70$ kPa
- **(b) Compacted clay:** $K = 100$ to $150$ kPa

Higher specific resistance indicates harder, more cohesive, or compacted soil requiring more energy per unit volume disturbed. **[3 marks]**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]
**Explain the significance of the Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion in soil cutting mechanics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion** states that soil fails along a plane when the shear stress on that plane reaches a critical value:
> $$\tau = c + \sigma \tan(\phi)$$
> In soil cutting, this criterion is used to: (i) predict the **angle of the rupture plane** (at $45° + \phi/2$ to the major principal stress direction), (ii) compute the **shear strength** that must be exceeded for tillage to occur, and (iii) determine the **passive earth pressure** acting on the tool face using $K_p = \tan^2(45° + \phi/2)$. Without this criterion, the geometry and force on a tillage tool cannot be predicted analytically. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**A mouldboard plough bottom of width $w = 0.30$ m is operated at depth $d = 0.20$ m in a soil with cohesion $c = 12$ kPa, angle of internal friction $\phi = 20°$, and unit weight $\gamma = 17.5$ kN/m³. The operating speed is $V = 1.8$ m/s. The Gill-Vanden Berg soil constants are $A = 38$ kPa, $B = 11$ kPa·s/m, $C = 1.8$ kPa·s²/m².**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the horizontal draft force using the passive wedge theory and calculate the draft on the plough using this model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the draft using the Gill-Vanden Berg equation and compare both results. Comment on the effect of speed. Also compute the power required. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

### Part (a) — Passive Wedge Theory (7 marks)

**Step 1 — State the Passive Wedge Equation.**

For a vertical, rough tillage tool, the horizontal draft per unit width in cohesionless soil is given by Rankine's passive earth pressure:
$$P_p = \frac{1}{2} \gamma d^2 K_p$$

**Step 2 — Add the Cohesion Term.**

Including cohesion, the modified passive resistance is:
$$P_p = \frac{1}{2} \gamma d^2 K_p + 2 c d \sqrt{K_p}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Passive Earth Pressure Coefficient.**

$$K_p = \tan^2\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right) = \tan^2(45° + 10°) = \tan^2(55°)$$
$$\tan(55°) = 1.4281 \Rightarrow K_p = (1.4281)^2 = 2.039$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Cohesion Term.**

$$2 c d \sqrt{K_p} = 2 \times 12 \times 0.20 \times \sqrt{2.039} = 4.8 \times 1.4281 = 6.855 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Friction Term.**

$$\frac{1}{2} \gamma d^2 K_p = \frac{1}{2} \times 17.5 \times (0.20)^2 \times 2.039 = 0.714 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 6 — Sum and Multiply by Width.**

$$P_p = (0.714 + 6.855) = 7.569 \text{ kN/m}$$
$$D = P_p \times w = 7.569 \times 0.30 = 2.27 \text{ kN}$$

**Part (a) Answer:** $D_{\text{passive}} \approx 2.27$ kN

---

### Part (b) — Gill-Vanden Berg and Comparison (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Apply the Gill-Vanden Berg Equation.**

$$D = (A + B V + C V^2) \cdot w \cdot d$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Values.**

$$A + BV + CV^2 = 38 + (11)(1.8) + (1.8)(1.8)^2$$
$$= 38 + 19.8 + (1.8)(3.24)$$
$$= 38 + 19.8 + 5.832 = 63.632 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by Cross-Sectional Area.**

$$A_c = w \cdot d = 0.30 \times 0.20 = 0.06 \text{ m}^2$$
$$D = 63.632 \times 0.06 = 3.82 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Power Required.**

$$P = D \cdot V = 3.82 \times 1.8 = 6.88 \text{ kW} \quad (\approx 9.22 \text{ hp})$$

**Step 5 — Comparison Table.**

| Method | Draft (kN) | Specific Resistance (kPa) |
|---|---|---|
| Passive Wedge (incl. cohesion) | 2.27 | 37.8 |
| Gill-Vanden Berg (at 1.8 m/s) | 3.82 | 63.6 |

**Step 6 — Comment on Speed Effect.**

The Gill-Vanden Berg draft (3.82 kN) is **68% higher** than the static passive wedge value (2.27 kN). This difference represents the **speed-dependent soil resistance** — primarily the viscous and inertial components. The equation shows that doubling the speed from 1.8 m/s to 3.6 m/s would increase the draft by approximately:
$$D_{2V} = (38 + 11 \times 3.6 + 1.8 \times 12.96) \times 0.06 = 100.13 \times 0.06 = 6.01 \text{ kN}$$
which is **57% higher**. Hence, speed has a significant non-linear effect on draft.

**Part (b) Answer:** $D_{\text{GVB}} = 3.82$ kN, $P = 6.88$ kW. Speed substantially increases draft due to the quadratic term $CV^2$. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [Alternative Choice — Internal Option]

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **Reece equation for two-dimensional soil failure** in front of a tillage tool. State the assumptions and derive an expression for the total horizontal force on a narrow blade of width $w$ and depth $d$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A subsoiler with a single shank is operated at 0.45 m depth in a clay-loam soil. The unit weight is $19$ kN/m³, $c = 25$ kPa, $\phi = 15°$. Calculate the draft force per unit width using (i) the passive wedge theory and (ii) the Reece model with $N_\gamma = 7.0$ and $N_c = 11.0$. Comment on which gives a more realistic estimate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

### Part (a) — Reece Equation Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Concept and Sketch Description.**

In Reece's (1965) model, the soil in front of a narrow tillage tool fails along a **log-spiral rupture surface** that begins at the tool tip and curves upward to the soil surface. This is more realistic than the straight Mohr-Coulomb plane. The model assumes:
- 2D plane strain (tool is long compared to its width)
- Soil is homogeneous and isotropic
- Soil-metal friction angle $\delta$ acts on the tool face
- Failure surface is a log-spiral

**Sketch Description (Textual Block Architecture):**
- A vertical line representing the tool face
- A curve (log-spiral) starting at the tool tip and arching up to the surface
- The soil wedge between the tool and the curve is the failure zone

**Step 2 — Mathematical Form.**

Reece's horizontal force on a wide blade per unit length:
$$D = w \left( \gamma g d^2 N_\gamma + c d N_c \right)$$

where $N_\gamma$ and $N_c$ are dimensionless factors obtained from log-spiral analysis depending on $\phi$, $\delta$, and the depth-to-width ratio.

**Step 3 — Note the Limitation.**

For very narrow tools (subsoilers, sweeps), 3D failure dominates, and Reece's 2D model over-predicts draft. Payne (1956) and Godwin-Spoor (1977) provide 3D corrections.

**Part (a) Answer:** Reece's 2D log-spiral model gives $D = w(\gamma g d^2 N_\gamma + c d N_c)$, more accurate than a straight Mohr-Coulomb plane. ✓

---

### Part (b) — Comparison Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute $K_p$ for Passive Wedge.**

$$K_p = \tan^2(45° + 15°/2) = \tan^2(52.5°) = (1.3032)^2 = 1.698$$

**Step 2 — Passive Wedge Draft (with cohesion).**

$$P_p = \frac{1}{2} \gamma d^2 K_p + 2 c d \sqrt{K_p}$$
$$= \frac{1}{2} \times 19 \times (0.45)^2 \times 1.698 + 2 \times 25 \times 0.45 \times \sqrt{1.698}$$
$$= 3.266 + 29.34 = 32.6 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 3 — Reece Model Draft.**

$$D_{\text{Reece}} = \gamma g d^2 N_\gamma + c d N_c$$

Note: Here $\gamma$ should be expressed in consistent units. Using $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³ directly:
$$D_{\text{Reece}} = (19)(0.45)^2 (7.0) + (25)(0.45)(11.0)$$
$$= 26.95 + 123.75 = 150.7 \text{ kN/m}$$

The Reece value is significantly higher. For a subsoiler, the actual measured draft is typically between these two — the passive wedge under-predicts (it ignores 3D effects and adhesion), and the Reece model over-predicts for very narrow shanks (where 3D side rupture reduces draft).

**Step 4 — Realistic Estimate.**

For a single subsoiler shank, a more realistic value is obtained by applying a **3D correction factor** (typically 0.4 to 0.6) to the Reece value:
$$D_{\text{realistic}} \approx 0.5 \times 150.7 \approx 75 \text{ kN}$$

This is more consistent with field measurements of 60–90 kN for subsoilers in clay-loam at 0.45 m depth.

**Part (b) Answer:** Passive wedge $\approx 32.6$ kN/m; Reece $\approx 150.7$ kN/m; realistic (3D corrected) $\approx 75$ kN/m. The Reece model is more conservative. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing $d$ in mm with $\gamma$ in kN/m³. Always convert to SI units: depths in **meters**, weights in **kN/m³**, forces in **kN**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Cohesion Term in Passive Wedge (–2 marks):** Many students use only $P_p = \frac{1}{2} \gamma d^2 K_p$ and ignore $c$. The full form is $P_p = \frac{1}{2} \gamma d^2 K_p + 2 c d \sqrt{K_p}$.
>
> 3. **Wrong Failure Plane Angle (–1 mark):** Writing $(45° - \phi/2)$ instead of $(45° + \phi/2)$ for **passive** failure. Remember: **active = $45° - \phi/2$; passive = $45° + \phi/2$**.
>
> 4. **Confusing $K_a$ and $K_p$ (–2 marks):** A retaining wall problem uses active $K_a = \tan^2(45° - \phi/2)$; a tillage tool pushes soil outward and uses **passive $K_p$**.
>
> 5. **Speed-Dependent Equation Errors (–2 marks):** Forgetting that the Gill-Vanden Berg equation has THREE terms: $A$, $BV$, and $CV^2$. Many students write only the linear term.
>
> 6. **No Sketch in Part B (–1 to 2 marks):** KTU examiners **expect a labeled diagram** of the soil wedge, tool, failure plane, and force vectors. A textual description alone is insufficient.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- [ ] **Soil cutting** is the controlled shear failure of soil by a wedge-shaped tool.
- [ ] **Three failure modes:** Brittle, Shear (wedge), Plastic (flow).
- [ ] **Mohr-Coulomb criterion:** $\tau = c + \sigma \tan(\phi)$ — the foundation equation.
- [ ] **Passive earth pressure coefficient:** $K_p = \tan^2(45° + \phi/2)$ — used for tillage tools.
- [ ] **Rupture plane angle:** $45° + \phi/2$ to the horizontal for passive failure.
- [ ] **Passive draft per unit width:** $P_p = \frac{1}{2}\gamma d^2 K_p + 2 c d \sqrt{K_p}$ (full form with cohesion).
- [ ] **Gill-Vanden Berg (most-used equation):** $D = (A + BV + CV^2) w d$.
- [ ] **ASAE simplified form:** $D = F_s \cdot w \cdot d$, where $F_s$ = specific resistance.
- [ ] **Specific resistance $K$:** $K = D / (w \cdot d)$, in kPa.
- [ ] **Tool angles:** $\alpha + \beta + \gamma + \delta = 90°$ (orthogonal 2D tool).
- [ ] **Higher speed = higher draft** (quadratic dependence at high $V$).
- [ ] **Higher depth = much higher draft** ($\propto d^2$ in passive model).
- [ ] **Reece (1965)** introduced the log-spiral failure model — more accurate than straight plane.
- [ ] **3D correction** is needed for narrow tools (subsoilers, chisels).
- [ ] **Unit weight** of soil: $\gamma = \rho_b \cdot g$ (typically 15–20 kN/m³).
- [ ] **Power required:** $P = D \times V$ in watts (or kW).
- [ ] **Always draw a labeled diagram** of the wedge, tool, and forces in 14-mark answers.
- [ ] **Conversion factor:** 1 kW = 1.341 hp; 1 hp = 0.7457 kW.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Passive Wedge + Mohr-Coulomb + Gill-Vanden Berg = the holy trinity of KTU tillage mechanics."* Memorize these three blocks of equations and you will secure at least **10/14 marks** on any Part B question from this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT743 - MECHANICS OF TILLAGE AND TRACTION > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Design Equation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the domain of **Mechanics of Tillage and Traction**, the term **Design Equation** refers to a mathematically rigorous, empirical-analytical expression that allows an engineer to **predict the draft force (horizontal pulling resistance)** required by an active or passive tillage tool operating at a specified working depth, width, and forward velocity through a defined soil condition. It is the foundational engineering relationship that bridges soil mechanics, tool geometry, and operational kinematics for the purpose of **matched implement–tractor system design**. In Module 2 of the KTU 2024 scheme, the principal "Design Equation" studied is the **ASABE Standard Draft Equation** (officially codified as **ASABE/ASAE Standard D497.7**), often supplemented by the **Universal Earthmoving Equation of Rowland and Pierce** and the **Bekker Pressure–Sinkage Equation**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEAGT743 – Module 2):** Students must master the **mathematical form**, **dimensional consistency**, **unit conversions** (SI ↔ field units), and the **physical meaning of every parameter** of the design equation. Numerical problems in the End Semester Examination (ESE) frequently test the calculation of draft force, specific draft resistance, and tractor power requirement. ### 1.2 The Standard Form (ASABE Formulation) The canonical form of the ASABE Draft Equation as taught in KTU Module 2 is: $$ D \;=\; F_i \,\left[\, A \;+\; B \cdot S \;+\; C \cdot S^{2} \,\right] \, W \, T $$ Where: - $D$ = **Draft force** (horizontal pull required by the implement), in Newtons (N) or kiloNewtons (kN). - $F_i$ = **Soil texture and moisture adjustment factor** (dimensionless ratio, tabular lookup). - $A$, $B$, $C$ = **Tool-specific regression coefficients** (dimensionless constants depending on tool geometry and base soil). - $S$ = **Forward travel speed** of the implement (in km/h, per ASABE convention). - $W$ = **Effective width of cut** (in metres, m). - $T$ = **Working depth of tillage** (in centimetres, cm — note the mixed-unit convention). > [!NOTE] > The peculiar mixed-unit convention (speed in km/h, depth in cm, width in m, draft in N) is **not an error**; it is the historical ASABE field convention preserved to retain consistency with decades of published agricultural machinery data. Students must take extreme care with unit conversions during KTU valuation. ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Pulling a Knife Through Clay" Imagine pushing a butter knife through a thick block of modelling clay: - **If you push slowly and shallow**, you barely feel any resistance. - **If you push the same knife deeper**, you must use much more force. - **If you push faster**, the resistance suddenly spikes because the clay cannot "get out of the way" fast enough — the material piles up and shears violently. - **If you use a wider knife** (more clay engaged at once), the force scales up roughly linearly. - **If the clay is wetter/stickier**, the resistance jumps again. The **Design Equation** mathematically captures *all five of these observations simultaneously*: | Physical Observation | Equation Parameter | |---|---| | Baseline resistance (even at zero speed) | $A$ | | Linear increase of resistance with speed | $B \cdot S$ | | Quadratic (parabolic) surge at high speeds | $C \cdot S^{2}$ | | Wider tool engages more soil | factor $W$ | | Deeper tool engages more soil volume | factor $T$ | | Different soil types resist differently | factor $F_i$ | This is the very essence of *matched system design*: given a soil, a tool, and a desired working depth, the engineer can compute the exact pull required, and then size the tractor (engine + ballast + tyres) accordingly. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Deconstruction of the Design Equation — Step-by-Step Logic The equation is **not** derived from a single first-principles law. It is a **semi-empirical regression model** that combines: 1. **Coulomb's Passive Earth Pressure Theory** (classical soil mechanics — gives the static, zero-speed component). 2. **Inertia and Strain-Rate Effects** in soil failure (gives the velocity-dependent terms, $B \cdot S$ and $C \cdot S^{2}$). 3. **Geometric Scaling Laws** (forces scale with the cross-sectional area of the soil prism being sheared — hence the multiplicative $W \cdot T$). Let us dissect the structure: **Step 1 — The Static Term ($A \cdot W \cdot T$):** The coefficient $A$ represents the **specific draft resistance per unit cross-sectional area at zero speed**, normalised by the soil-texture factor $F_i$. It captures the Coulombic shearing of the soil prism in front of the tool tip, including: - Soil cohesion ($c$) - Internal angle of friction ($\phi$) - Soil–metal adhesion ($c_a$) - Tool rake angle geometry **Step 2 — The Linear Velocity Term ($B \cdot S \cdot W \cdot T$):** This term models the **visco-plastic strain-rate effect**. Real soils behave partially like a Bingham fluid — at low speeds the resistance grows linearly with the rate of strain. This is dominant at typical field speeds (5–8 km/h). **Step 3 — The Quadratic Velocity Term ($C \cdot S^{2} \cdot W \cdot T$):** This term captures the **inertial and dynamic soil acceleration effects**. As speed rises, the soil mass in front of the tool must be physically accelerated and thrown aside, creating an additional drag proportional to the *kinetic energy* (hence $S^{2}$). This becomes dominant above ~9 km/h. **Step 4 — The Geometric Multiplier ($W \cdot T$):** The cross-sectional area of the soil failure prism. Wider/deeper tools engage more soil → more draft, with **strong linear scaling** in most field conditions (the equation assumes homogeneous soil and constant tool shape). **Step 5 — The Soil Factor ($F_i$):** A tabulated dimensionless multiplier between roughly 0.65 and 1.50 that adjusts the "base soil" (typically a sandy loam at optimum moisture) to the actual field soil and moisture condition. KTU examinations often provide a small lookup table for $F_i$. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table) > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** This table contains every equation you are likely to need in Module 2 numericals. Memorise the parameter meanings — *not* the numerical values, since the question paper always supplies a new data set. | # | Equation / Relationship | Mathematical Form | Typical Units | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **ASABE Draft Equation** (Master Design Eqn.) | $D = F_i \, [\, A + B \cdot S + C \cdot S^{2} \,] \, W \, T$ | $N$ | Horizontal pull on tillage tool | | 2 | **Specific Draft Resistance** (SDR) | $SDR = \dfrac{D}{W \cdot T}$ | $kPa$ or $N/m^{2}$ | Draft per unit soil cross-section | | 3 | **Drawbar Power Required** | $P_{db} = D \cdot v = \dfrac{D \cdot S}{3.6}$ | $W$ | Power at implement hitch point | | 4 | **Conversion: Speed km/h → m/s** | $v = \dfrac{S}{3.6}$ | $m/s$ | Velocity unit bridge | | 5 | **Conversion: Depth cm → m** | $T_{m} = \dfrac{T_{cm}}{100}$ | $m$ | Unit consistency check | | 6 | **Universal Earthmoving Eqn. (Rowland–Pierce)** | $H = \left( \dfrac{W}{T} \right) \cdot \gamma \cdot \dfrac{N_{\gamma} - 1}{2} \cdot T^{2} \cdot K_{p} + \gamma \cdot T^{2} \cdot N_{c} \cdot c \cdot K_{c}$ | $N/m$ | Horizontal force per unit length on a wide blade | | 7 | **Bekker Plate Sinkage Eqn. (Rectangular)** | $p = \left( \dfrac{k_{c}}{b} + k_{\phi} \right) \cdot z^{n}$ | $kPa$ | Pressure under a sinkage plate | | 8 | **Bekker Plate Sinkage Eqn. (Circular)** | $p = \dfrac{k_{c}}{b} + k_{\phi} \cdot z^{n}$ | $kPa$ | Pressure under a circular sinkage plate | | 9 | **Compaction Pressure from Wheel** | $p_{c} = \left( \dfrac{k_{c}}{b} + k_{\phi} \right) \cdot z^{n}$ | $kPa$ | Contact pressure for traction analysis | | 10 | **Wheel Numeric Sinkage Power** | $p = k_{c} \cdot z^{n}$ (limit $b \to \infty$) | $kPa$ | Simplified for very wide plates | > [!WARNING] > In the tables above, every absolute-value or division symbol has been written as `\vert` or `/` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Do **not** blindly retype `|` into your answer sheet's tables during exam preparation. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The Design Equation is the **backbone of tractor-implement matching**: - **Tractor Manufacturer** (e.g., Mahindra, John Deere, TAFE): uses it to specify **minimum drawbar horsepower** for a given plough, harrow, or cultivator. - **Farm Manager**: calculates the **number of tractors** and the **working width** needed to till a 100-hectare field within a 7-day window. - **Soil Conservation Engineer**: predicts how a deeper cut (say, deep ploughing at 30 cm vs. normal at 15 cm) will spike draft and possibly exceed the tractor's pull capacity — risking wheel slip. - **Implement Designer**: uses the regression coefficients $A$, $B$, $C$ as **design targets** when prototyping new share, disc, or tine geometries. In short, **the design equation translates soil physics into a horsepower number** — the universal currency of agricultural machinery. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Physical Derivation of the Design Equation (Conceptual Chain) The ASABE Draft Equation is empirical; however, its mathematical **structure** can be motivated from first principles. The derivation chain used in KTU Module 2 lectures proceeds as follows: **Starting Postulate — Passive Earth Pressure on a Wide Blade:** For a wide, flat, vertical blade pushed horizontally at constant depth through a weightless, cohesive-frictional soil, classical Coulomb theory gives the horizontal force per unit blade length as: $$ H \;=\; \gamma \cdot T^{2} \cdot N_{\gamma} \cdot K_{p} \;+\; c \cdot T \cdot N_{c} \cdot K_{c} $$ where $\gamma$ is soil unit weight, $T$ is depth, $N_{\gamma}$ and $N_{c}$ are dimensionless bearing-capacity factors, and $K_{p}$, $K_{c}$ are dimensionless multipliers from passive earth pressure theory. For simplicity, group all the dimensionless coefficients into a single **base coefficient** $K_{0}$ that depends only on $\phi$, $c$, $\gamma$, and blade geometry. Then: $$ H \;=\; K_{0} \, T^{2} $$ **Step 1 — Converting to Force (not per-unit-length) by introducing Width:** The blade is not infinitely wide. For a tool of width $W$, the total horizontal force becomes: $$ F_{static} \;=\; H \cdot W \;=\; K_{0} \cdot W \cdot T^{2} $$ But experimental data (from ASAE field trials) shows a more linear dependence on $T$. To align theory with data, we normalise the equation differently. Define a new **specific static coefficient** $A'$ such that: $$ F_{static} \;=\; A' \cdot W \cdot T $$ This coefficient $A'$ is what gets tabulated in ASABE tables as coefficient $A$ (after multiplying by $F_i$). **Step 2 — Adding Velocity-Dependent Terms:** Experimental curves of draft vs. speed are gently parabolic. A second-order Taylor expansion of the draft $D$ around zero speed gives: $$ D(S) \;\approx\; D(0) \;+\; \left. \dfrac{\partial D}{\partial S} \right\vert_{0} \cdot S \;+\; \dfrac{1}{2} \left. \dfrac{\partial^{2} D}{\partial S^{2}} \right\vert_{0} \cdot S^{2} $$ Substituting the static term from Step 1 and renaming the partial derivatives as $B'$ and $C'$: $$ D(S) \;=\; A' \cdot W \cdot T \;+\; B' \cdot S \cdot W \cdot T \;+\; C' \cdot S^{2} \cdot W \cdot T $$ **Step 3 — Factoring Out the Geometric Term and Inserting the Soil Factor:** Pull out $W \cdot T$ and define the soil-texture adjustment factor $F_i$ to convert from the reference (base) soil to the actual field soil: $$ D \;=\; F_i \, [\, A \;+\; B \cdot S \;+\; C \cdot S^{2} \,] \, W \, T $$ This is the **complete ASABE design equation** as used in KTU examinations. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU-Style 3-Mark Style Computation > **Problem Statement:** > A mounted mouldboard plough has the following ASABE parameters: $A = 350$, $B = 18$, $C = 0.85$, $F_i = 1.10$. The plough operates at a width $W = 1.2$ m, depth $T = 20$ cm, and forward speed $S = 7$ km/h. > **Required:** (a) Draft force in kN. (b) Specific draft resistance in kPa. (c) Drawbar power in kW. **Solution (Step-by-Step Exhaustive Expansion):** **Part (a) — Draft Force:** Step 1: Substitute into the design equation: $$ D \;=\; F_i \, [\, A \;+\; B \cdot S \;+\; C \cdot S^{2} \,] \, W \, T $$ Step 2: Compute the term inside the brackets (the velocity polynomial): $$ B \cdot S \;=\; 18 \times 7 \;=\; 126 $$ $$ C \cdot S^{2} \;=\; 0.85 \times 7^{2} \;=\; 0.85 \times 49 \;=\; 41.65 $$ $$ A \;+\; B \cdot S \;+\; C \cdot S^{2} \;=\; 350 \;+\; 126 \;+\; 41.65 \;=\; 517.65 $$ Step 3: Multiply by $W$ and $T$: $$ W \cdot T \;=\; 1.2 \times 20 \;=\; 24 \; \text{m·cm} $$ Step 4: Apply $F_i$: $$ D \;=\; 1.10 \times 517.65 \times 24 $$ $$ D \;=\; 1.10 \times 12{,}423.6 $$ $$ \boxed{D \;=\; 13{,}665.96 \; \text{N} \;\approx\; 13.67 \; \text{kN}} $$ **Part (b) — Specific Draft Resistance:** $$ SDR \;=\; \dfrac{D}{W \cdot T_{m}} $$ where $T_{m} = 20/100 = 0.20$ m. So $W \cdot T_{m} = 1.2 \times 0.20 = 0.24$ m$^{2}$. $$ SDR \;=\; \dfrac{13{,}665.96}{0.24} \;=\; 56{,}941.5 \; \text{N/m}^{2} $$ $$ \boxed{SDR \;\approx\; 56.94 \; \text{kPa}} $$ **Part (c) — Drawbar Power:** Convert speed: $v = S / 3.6 = 7 / 3.6 = 1.944$ m/s. $$ P_{db} \;=\; D \cdot v \;=\; 13{,}665.96 \times 1.944 $$ $$ P_{db} \;=\; 26{,}566.6 \; \text{W} \;\approx\; 26.57 \; \text{kW} $$ $$ \boxed{P_{db} \;\approx\; 26.57 \; \text{kW}} $$ ### 3.3 Dimensional Analysis Sanity Check (Examiner's Favourite) The dimensions of the bracket $[A + B \cdot S + C \cdot S^{2}]$ must all be the same, because they are being **added**. We verify: $$ [A] \;=\; \text{(dimensionless coefficient, since } F_i \text{ absorbs the unit)} $$ Actually, deeper dimensional analysis: $A$, $B$, $C$ together form a polynomial in $S$, so: - $A$ has units of $1$ (in the ASABE empirical system) - $B \cdot S$ has units of $1$ ⇒ $B$ has units of $h/km$ - $C \cdot S^{2}$ has units of $1$ ⇒ $C$ has units of $h^{2}/km^{2}$ The product $[A + B \cdot S + C \cdot S^{2}] \cdot W \cdot T$ yields a number with units of m·cm, which when multiplied by $F_i$ (dimensionless) gives the **dimensioned draft force in Newtons** only because the tabulated coefficients $A$, $B$, $C$ are **pre-calibrated for SI output** when $W$ is in m, $T$ in cm, $S$ in km/h. > [!IMPORTANT] > This is why students **must not** re-derive $A$, $B$, $C$ from a different data set without first confirming the unit convention used in that data set. KTU exam questions always state units explicitly. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Design Equation System The diagram below maps the **functional flow** of information and parameters from raw soil/tool inputs to the final engineering outputs (draft, power, tractor match). ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Soil Input Layer
Type and Texture] --> nodeB[Lookup Table for F sub i
Soil Texture Adjustment Factor] nodeC[Tool Geometry Layer
Width W, Depth T, Shape] --> nodeG[Cross Sectional Area
W times T] nodeD[Operational Layer
Forward Speed S in km per h] --> nodeE[Velocity Polynomial
A plus B S plus C S squared] nodeB --> nodeF[Soil Adjusted Bracket
F sub i times Polynomial] nodeE --> nodeF nodeG --> nodeH[Final Draft Equation
D equals Bracket times W times T] nodeF --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI[Draft Force Output
D in Newtons] nodeI --> nodeJ[Derived Outputs
Specific Draft and Drawbar Power] nodeJ --> nodeK[Tractor Implement Match
Power and Ballast Decision] style nodeA fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD style nodeD fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100 style nodeC fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F style nodeH fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457 style nodeK fill:#EDE7F6,stroke:#4527A0 ``` ### 4.2 Schematic — Force Vectors on a Tillage Tool ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeT[Tractor Hitch Point] -->|Direction of Travel| nodeV[Velocity Vector V] nodeTool[Mouldboard or Tine
at Depth T and Width W] --> nodeD[Horizontal Draft D
along travel direction] nodeTool --> nodeVS[Vertical Soil Reaction V sub s
acting upward] nodeSoil[Soil Failure Prism
forward of tool] --> nodeD nodeSoil --> nodeVS style nodeT fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0 style nodeTool fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17 style nodeSoil fill:#EFEBE9,stroke:#4E342E style nodeD fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C style nodeVS fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20 ``` ### 4.3 Functional Interaction Map — How the Equation Connects Sub-Disciplines ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeSM[Classical Soil Mechanics
Coulomb Failure Theory] --> nodeDE[Design Equation
D equals F sub i bracket W T] nodeVE[Vehicle Mechanics
Bekker Pressure Sinkage] --> nodeDE nodeEM[Experimental Field Trials
ASABE Regression Data] --> nodeDE nodeDE --> nodeTM[Tractor Sizing
Engine and Ballast] nodeDE --> nodeID[Implement Design
Share and Disc Geometry] nodeDE --> nodeFC[Field Capacity Planning
Hectares per Day] style nodeSM fill:#E1F5FE style nodeVE fill:#FFF3E0 style nodeEM fill:#F3E5F5 style nodeTM fill:#E8F5E9 style nodeID fill:#FCE4EC style nodeFC fill:#FFFDE7 ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagrams above intentionally abstract the **physical geometry** of a plough and instead show the **information flow** between subsystems. This is the preferred KTU Module 2 schematic style because the design equation itself is a *model*, not a *machine* — its logic is best captured as a flow, not as a drawing. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** **Define the term "Specific Draft Resistance" as applied to tillage implements. State its SI unit and explain its physical significance to a farmer.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Specific Draft Resistance (SDR)** is defined as the draft force required per unit cross-sectional area of the soil slice being cut. Mathematically: > > $$ > SDR \;=\; \dfrac{D}{W \cdot T_{m}} > $$ > > Its **SI unit is the Pascal (Pa)** or, more practically, **kiloPascal (kPa)** or **N/m²**. > > **Physical significance:** A *low* SDR means the soil is loose, dry, and easy to work (e.g., a sandy loam at optimum moisture may have an SDR of 30–40 kPa). A *high* SDR means compact, wet, or clayey soil (saturated clay may exceed 100 kPa). For the farmer, SDR directly tells how much **fuel and horsepower** will be consumed per hectare, and whether their existing tractor can handle a deeper tillage operation. It is also used to **compare tillage tools** — a well-designed subsoiler should give a lower SDR than a disc plough at the same depth. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]** **List any three factors that affect the draft force of a tillage implement and state how each factor quantitatively enters the ASABE design equation.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Factor | Entry Point in Design Equation | Quantitative Effect | |---|---|---| | **Soil type and moisture** | $F_i$ factor | $F_i$ varies from ~0.65 (sandy, dry) to ~1.50 (clayey, wet); multiplies entire draft | | **Forward travel speed** | $S$ inside bracket $A + B \cdot S + C \cdot S^{2}$ | Doubling speed can increase draft by 30–60% due to the $B \cdot S$ and $C \cdot S^{2}$ terms | | **Working depth** | $T$ as a multiplicative factor | Doubling depth doubles draft (linear proportionality for most soils) | *(Any three valid factors with correct equation references receive full 3 marks.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Speed and Depth Sensitivity Analysis** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Question Paper]** **(a)** Derive, from first principles, the **physical structure** of the ASABE Draft Equation, clearly identifying the origin of the static term, the linear velocity term, and the quadratic velocity term. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 3-bottom mounted mouldboard plough is operated at a depth of 18 cm and a width of 1.05 m. The ASABE parameters are: $A = 290$, $B = 16$, $C = 0.78$, and the soil-texture factor $F_i = 0.95$. The tractor initially pulls the plough at **6 km/h**, and the operator wishes to increase speed to **9 km/h** to complete the field before evening. Compute: - (i) The draft force at 6 km/h and at 9 km/h. **(3 marks)** - (ii) The percentage increase in draft force. **(2 marks)** - (iii) The drawbar power required at 9 km/h, in kW. **(2 marks)** **Model Answer — Part (a) (7 marks):** The derivation begins with Coulomb's classical passive earth pressure theory, which states that for a wide, vertical blade pushed horizontally through a weightless, cohesive-frictional soil at constant depth, the horizontal force per unit blade length is: $$ H \;=\; \gamma \cdot T^{2} \cdot N_{\gamma} \cdot K_{p} \;+\; c \cdot T \cdot N_{c} \cdot K_{c} $$ All the dimensionless bearing-capacity and pressure coefficients ($N_{\gamma}$, $N_{c}$, $K_{p}$, $K_{c}$) are grouped into a single tool-and-soil-dependent constant $K_{0}$ for brevity. For a tool of finite width $W$, the total static force becomes: $$ F_{static} \;=\; K_{0} \cdot W \cdot T^{2} $$ However, ASABE field data shows that the dependence on $T$ is closer to linear than quadratic when the tool geometry includes a properly designed share that breaks the soil prism cleanly. To align the theoretical model with experimental observation, the static term is empirically restructured as: $$ F_{static} \;=\; A' \cdot W \cdot T $$ This defines the **specific static coefficient** $A'$. **(2 marks for derivation up to this point.)** Now consider the effect of forward speed. Experimentally, draft grows approximately parabolically with speed. A second-order Taylor expansion of $D(S)$ around $S = 0$ yields: $$ D(S) \;\approx\; D(0) \;+\; \left. \dfrac{\partial D}{\partial S} \right\vert_{0} \cdot S \;+\; \dfrac{1}{2} \left. \dfrac{\partial^{2} D}{\partial S^{2}} \right\vert_{0} \cdot S^{2} $$ Substituting $D(0) = A' \cdot W \cdot T$ and renaming the partial derivatives as $B'$ and $C'$: $$ D(S) \;=\; A' \cdot W \cdot T \;+\; B' \cdot S \cdot W \cdot T \;+\; C' \cdot S^{2} \cdot W \cdot T $$ The first velocity-dependent term $B' \cdot S$ arises from the **visco-plastic strain-rate sensitivity** of the soil — the soil behaves partially as a Bingham fluid, so resistance grows linearly with strain rate at moderate speeds. The quadratic term $C' \cdot S^{2}$ arises from the **inertial acceleration of the displaced soil mass** — at higher speeds, the soil must be physically accelerated and thrown aside, and this kinetic-energy demand scales as $S^{2}$. **(3 marks for velocity-term physics.)** Finally, the soil-texture factor $F_i$ converts the reference (base sandy loam) coefficients to the actual field soil, and we obtain: $$ D \;=\; F_i \, [\, A \;+\; B \cdot S \;+\; C \cdot S^{2} \,] \, W \cdot T $$ where $A \equiv A'$, $B \equiv B'$, $C \equiv C'$ are the tabulated ASABE coefficients for a specific tool geometry in a reference soil. **(2 marks for the final assembly and identification of physical origin of each term.)** **Model Answer — Part (b) (7 marks):** **(i) Draft at 6 km/h:** Compute the velocity polynomial: $$ A + B \cdot S + C \cdot S^{2} \;=\; 290 + (16)(6) + (0.78)(6^{2}) $$ $$ = 290 + 96 + (0.78)(36) $$ $$ = 290 + 96 + 28.08 \;=\; 414.08 $$ Compute $W \cdot T = 1.05 \times 18 = 18.9$ m·cm. $$ D_{6} \;=\; 0.95 \times 414.08 \times 18.9 \;=\; 0.95 \times 7{,}826.112 \;=\; 7{,}434.81 \; \text{N} $$ $$ \boxed{D_{6} \;\approx\; 7.43 \; \text{kN}} $$ **(i) Draft at 9 km/h:** $$ A + B \cdot S + C \cdot S^{2} \;=\; 290 + (16)(9) + (0.78)(9^{2}) $$ $$ = 290 + 144 + (0.78)(81) $$ $$ = 290 + 144 + 63.18 \;=\; 497.18 $$ $$ D_{9} \;=\; 0.95 \times 497.18 \times 18.9 \;=\; 0.95 \times 9{,}396.702 \;=\; 8{,}926.87 \; \text{N} $$ $$ \boxed{D_{9} \;\approx\; 8.93 \; \text{kN}} $$ **(ii) Percentage Increase in Draft:** $$ \% \; \text{Increase} \;=\; \dfrac{D_{9} - D_{6}}{D_{6}} \times 100 \;=\; \dfrac{8{,}926.87 - 7{,}434.81}{7{,}434.81} \times 100 $$ $$ = \dfrac{1{,}492.06}{7{,}434.81} \times 100 \;\approx\; 20.07\% $$ $$ \boxed{\% \text{Increase} \;\approx\; 20.1\%} $$ **(iii) Drawbar Power at 9 km/h:** Convert speed: $v = 9 / 3.6 = 2.5$ m/s. $$ P_{db} \;=\; D_{9} \cdot v \;=\; 8{,}926.87 \times 2.5 \;=\; 22{,}317.18 \; \text{W} $$ $$ \boxed{P_{db} \;\approx\; 22.32 \; \text{kW}} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Bekker Equation and Tractor Match** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]** **(a)** Explain the **Bekker Pressure–Sinkage Equation** for a rectangular footing plate. Define each parameter, state its units, and describe how the equation is used to compute the rolling resistance of a tractor wheel. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A tractor wheel is approximated as a rectangular footing of width $b = 0.40$ m. Bekker's soil parameters are: $k_c = 12$ kN/m$^{(n+1)}$, $k_{\phi} = 600$ kN/m$^{(n+2)}$, $n = 0.8$. Under static load, the wheel sinks by $z = 0.06$ m. Compute: - (i) The contact pressure $p$ under the plate using Bekker's equation. **(3 marks)** - (ii) The total vertical load the soil can support at this sinkage (assume the contact patch length equals the wheel radius 0.55 m). **(2 marks)** - (iii) The rolling resistance if the coefficient of rolling resistance $\rho = 0.18$ (explain briefly what this coefficient physically represents). **(2 marks)** **Model Answer — Part (a) (7 marks):** The **Bekker Pressure–Sinkage Equation** is an empirical model that predicts the **vertical pressure $p$** a soil exerts under a flat plate as a function of the plate's **sinkage depth $z$**. For a **rectangular plate** of width $b$, Bekker's equation is: $$ p \;=\; \left( \dfrac{k_{c}}{b} \;+\; k_{\phi} \right) \, z^{n} $$ **Parameter Definitions and Units:** - $p$ = **contact pressure** beneath the plate (in kPa or kN/m²). - $b$ = **smaller dimension of the contact plate** (in m). For a long rectangular plate this is the short side; for a square or circular plate it is the diameter. - $z$ = **vertical sinkage** of the plate into the soil (in m). - $n$ = **dimensionless sinkage exponent**, typically ranging from 0.5 (hard soil) to 1.2 (very soft, freshly tilled soil). - $k_c$ = **cohesive modulus of soil** (in kN/m$^{(n+1)}$) — captures the soil's resistance to deformation due to cohesion. - $k_{\phi}$ = **frictional modulus of soil** (in kN/m$^{(n+2)}$) — captures the soil's resistance due to internal friction. The ratio $k_c / b$ has the same units as $k_{\phi}$ (i.e., kN/m$^{(n+2)}$) because $b$ has units of m, so $k_c / b$ becomes kN/m$^{(n+1)} \cdot 1/\text{m}$ = kN/m$^{(n+2)}$. The two terms are then added and multiplied by $z^{n}$ (dimensionless × m$^n$) to give kN/m² = kPa. **(3 marks for the equation and parameter definitions.)** **Application to Rolling Resistance of a Tractor Wheel:** When a driven or towed tractor wheel rolls over soft soil, the wheel sinks by depth $z$ and the contact patch between tyre and soil is approximated as a rectangular footing of width $b$ (the tyre width) and contact length $l$ (often approximated as the wheel radius for soft soils). The Bekker equation gives the **average pressure $p$** under this patch. The **vertical load** supported by the soil is then $W = p \cdot b \cdot l$, and the **rolling resistance** is the horizontal force needed to overcome the energy dissipated in compressing and shearing the soil: $$ R_{r} \;=\; \rho \cdot W $$ where $\rho$ is the **coefficient of rolling resistance** (typically 0.05–0.30 for agricultural tyres on soft soil). Equivalently, $R_r$ can be derived by integrating the Bekker pressure over the contact patch as the wheel climbs out of the rut. **(4 marks for the rolling-resistance derivation and physical interpretation.)** **Model Answer — Part (b) (7 marks):** **(i) Contact Pressure:** $$ p \;=\; \left( \dfrac{k_{c}}{b} \;+\; k_{\phi} \right) \, z^{n} $$ Compute the term inside the parentheses: $$ \dfrac{k_{c}}{b} \;=\; \dfrac{12}{0.40} \;=\; 30 \; \text{kN/m}^{(n+2)} $$ $$ \dfrac{k_{c}}{b} + k_{\phi} \;=\; 30 + 600 \;=\; 630 \; \text{kN/m}^{(n+2)} $$ Compute $z^{n}$: $$ z^{n} \;=\; (0.06)^{0.8} $$ Using logarithms: $\ln(0.06) = -2.813$; multiplied by $0.8$: $-2.251$; exponentiated: $e^{-2.251} \approx 0.1053$. So $z^{n} \approx 0.1053$ m$^{0.8}$. Therefore: $$ p \;=\; 630 \times 0.1053 \;\approx\; 66.34 \; \text{kPa} $$ $$ \boxed{p \;\approx\; 66.34 \; \text{kPa}} $$ **(ii) Total Vertical Load Supported:** $$ W \;=\; p \cdot b \cdot l \;=\; 66.34 \times 0.40 \times 0.55 $$ $$ W \;=\; 66.34 \times 0.22 \;=\; 14.595 \; \text{kN} $$ $$ \boxed{W \;\approx\; 14.60 \; \text{kN}} $$ **(iii) Rolling Resistance:** $$ R_{r} \;=\; \rho \cdot W \;=\; 0.18 \times 14.60 $$ $$ \boxed{R_{r} \;\approx\; 2.63 \; \text{kN}} $$ **Physical Meaning of $\rho$:** The coefficient of rolling resistance $\rho$ is a dimensionless ratio that represents the **moment arm** (as a fraction of the wheel radius) at which the resultant soil reaction acts behind the wheel centre. A value of 0.18 means that, as the wheel rolls, the average soil pressure acts effectively at 18% of the wheel radius behind the wheel's vertical centre line. This horizontal offset creates a **resistive moment** that the tractor must overcome to keep the wheel turning — this is the rolling resistance. **(2 marks for the interpretation.)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Module 2 Design Equation Problems:** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap:** Students frequently mix $W$ in metres with $T$ in centimetres, get a numerically large draft, and forget to convert to kN. The KTU scheme expects **final answers in kN**, not N. > 2. **Speed Conversion Omission:** Drawing power $P = D \cdot v$ requires $v$ in **m/s**, not km/h. Dividing by 3.6 is the most-skipped step in valuation scripts. > 3. **Bracket vs. Multiplicative Mis-grouping:** Students often write $D = A + B \cdot S + C \cdot S^{2} \cdot W \cdot T \cdot F_i$, forgetting that the **entire velocity polynomial is multiplied by $W \cdot T \cdot F_i$**. This gives grossly under-estimated draft and an immediate 2-mark deduction. > 4. **Soil Factor $F_i$ Forgetting:** Roughly 40% of the class omits $F_i$ entirely in viva. It is a **multiplicative factor on the entire bracket**, not an additive correction. > 5. **Bekker Equation Confusion:** Many students confuse the **rectangular** Bekker form ($k_c/b + k_{\phi}$) with the **circular** form ($k_c/b + k_{\phi}$, but interpreted differently with $b$ = diameter). For a circular plate, the pressure at the centre is governed by the *sum*, but $b$ is the diameter. Always check the figure caption. > 6. **Polynomial Non-Dimensionality:** In the design equation, $A$, $B$, $C$ are **not** dimensionless — they have hidden units ($h/km$ for $B$, $h^{2}/km^{2}$ for $C$) absorbed via the SI calibration. Do not attempt a strict Buckingham-$\pi$ dimensional analysis unless you convert $S$ to m/s first. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: Design Equation** - **Master Equation:** $D = F_i \, [\, A + B \cdot S + C \cdot S^{2} \,] \, W \, T$ - **Units Convention (ASABE):** $S$ in **km/h**, $W$ in **m**, $T$ in **cm**, $D$ in **N** (convert to kN for reporting). - **Soil Factor $F_i$:** Dimensionless; typically **0.65 to 1.50**; multiply the **entire velocity polynomial** by it. - **Three Physical Origins:** Static (Coulombic shearing) → term $A$; Linear velocity (visco-plastic strain rate) → term $B \cdot S$; Quadratic velocity (soil inertia) → term $C \cdot S^{2}$. - **Specific Draft Resistance:** $SDR = D / (W \cdot T_{m})$, in **Pa or kPa**. - **Drawbar Power:** $P_{db} = D \cdot v$, with $v = S / 3.6$ (convert km/h to m/s). - **Bekker Pressure–Sinkage Equation:** $p = (k_c / b + k_{\phi}) \, z^{n}$ — governs **tyre–soil interaction** and wheel rolling resistance. - **Coefficient of Rolling Resistance $\rho$:** Typically **0.05 to 0.30**; $R_r = \rho \cdot W$ where $W$ is dynamic wheel load. - **Dimensional Rule:** The velocity polynomial inside the brackets is dimensionless *only when $A$, $B$, $C$ are read from the ASABE tables for the stated units of $S$*. Do not mix data sets. - **Practical Rule of Thumb (Memory Aid):** Draft approximately **doubles** when depth doubles; draft increases **20–60%** when speed increases from 6 to 9 km/h on medium soils. - **Common Numerical Pitfall:** Always convert $T$ from cm to m when computing $SDR$ or contact patch area. - **Tractor Matching Logic:** Drawbar power + rolling resistance power ≤ ~85% of engine flywheel power (rule of thumb for traction-limited operation). ---

topics.title: Force Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT743 - MECHANICS OF TILLAGE AND TRACTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Force Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Force Analysis in Tillage**: The systematic study and quantification of all mechanical forces acting between a soil-engaging tool (tine, disc, share, or blade) and the soil mass during tillage operations. It involves the resolution, decomposition, and resultant computation of horizontal draft, vertical penetration, and side-draft components required to predict implement power requirements, traction device loading, and structural design of tillage machinery.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework for *PEAGT743 – Mechanics of Tillage and Traction*, **Module 2 (Force Analysis)** establishes the mathematical and physical basis for understanding:

- The **soil–tool interaction mechanics** that govern implement performance
- The **resolution of forces** acting on shares, discs, tines, and sweeps
- The **prediction of draft resistance** for design of matching tractor power units
- The **evaluation of soil failure patterns** (shear, tensile, compression) under tool loading

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "Cutting Butter with a Knife"**
> Imagine pushing a knife through a cold block of butter. The harder you push forward (**horizontal draft force**), the deeper the knife sinks. If you tilt the knife upward, the **vertical force** either pulls the implement into the soil (positive penetration) or pushes it out (negative). If you tilt the knife sideways, the butter "drags" the knife to one side (**side draft**). The **resistance** you feel is the *resultant* of three internal forces inside the butter — these are identical to the soil reaction components a farmer's plough experiences in the field.

Think of a tractor plough as a wedge forcing its way through soil:

- **Soil** = visco-elasto-plastic medium (neither perfectly elastic like rubber nor perfectly plastic like wet clay — it is a *combination*)
- **Tillage tool** = a wedge that must overcome **soil cohesion (c)** and **internal friction angle (φ)**
- **Tractor** = a force source providing **drawbar pull** to overcome **draft resistance**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):** Students are expected to derive draft force equations using classical soil mechanics (Coulomb's failure criterion, Mohr-Coulomb theory), apply **dimensional analysis**, and predict the **specific resistance** of various tillage tools. The standard metric used in KTU/ASAE design is **specific resistance = Draft / (Width × Depth)**, typically expressed in **kPa (kN/m²)**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Standard SI Units)

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $D$ | Draft (horizontal) force | **kN** |
| $V$ | Vertical force | **kN** |
| $L$ | Side (lateral) draft force | **kN** |
| $R$ | Resultant soil reaction | **kN** |
| $c$ | Soil cohesion | **kPa** |
| $\phi$ | Soil internal friction angle | **degrees (°)** |
| $\rho_b$ | Soil bulk density | **kg/m³** |
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** |
| $\alpha$ | Tool rake angle | **degrees (°)** |
| $w$ | Tool width | **m** |
| $d$ | Depth of operation | **m** |
| $v$ | Forward velocity | **m/s** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** Every tillage design problem in KTU university examinations begins with the *correct identification* of these primary force components. Missing even one (e.g., side draft) costs 2–3 marks in the valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Soil Failure Theory (Mohr–Coulomb Criterion)

The **Mohr–Coulomb failure criterion** is the bedrock of all KTU tillage force problems. It states that **shear failure** in soil occurs when the shear stress ($\tau$) on any plane reaches a critical combination of **cohesion** and **frictional resistance**:

$$\tau = c + \sigma \tan\phi$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = shear strength of soil at failure (kPa)
- $c$ = apparent cohesion of soil (kPa)
- $\sigma$ = normal stress on the failure plane (kPa)
- $\phi$ = angle of internal friction (°)
- $\tan\phi$ = coefficient of internal friction (dimensionless)

### The "Why" Behind Mohr–Coulomb
- Soil grains are in contact at micro-asperities → these provide **frictional resistance** proportional to $\sigma$
- Soil particles are also bonded by **electro-chemical and capillary forces** → these provide **cohesion** independent of $\sigma$
- The total shear strength is the **sum** of these two mechanisms

## 2.2 Coulomb's Earth Pressure Theory Applied to Tillage

When a tillage tool moves through soil, it can be idealized as a **smooth or rough retaining wall** moving relative to a soil mass. Two classical cases arise:

### Case 1: Soil Failure by Shear (Passive Failure — Ahead of Tool)
The soil mass **in front of the tool** is pushed forward and upward. The failure surface is approximately **logarithmic-spiral**, but KTU problems simplify it to a **planar failure wedge** at angle $(45° + \phi/2)$ to the horizontal.

### Case 2: Soil Failure by Tension (Brittle Failure — Behind Narrow Tools)
For **narrow tools** (subsoilers, sweeps, knife-type coulters), the soil fails by **tensile fracture** along curved failure surfaces behind the tool.

## 2.3 Components of Total Draft Force

The total draft force $D$ on a tillage tool is generally resolved into **three sub-components** according to KTU 2024 syllabus:

$$D = D_c + D_a + D_f$$

| Component | Description | Physical Origin |
|---|---|---|
| $D_c$ | Cohesion component | Bonding between soil particles |
| $D_a$ | Adhesion component | Soil-to-metal sticking |
| $D_f$ | Friction component | Soil-to-soil internal friction + tool friction |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — it covers 80% of KTU Part B Module 2 numericals.**

| # | Equation | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = c + \sigma \tan\phi$ | Mohr–Coulomb shear strength | kPa |
| 2 | $D = K_c \, c \, w \, d + K_\phi \, \rho_b \, g \, w \, d^{2}$ | Universal draft equation (Reece) | kN |
| 3 | $R = \sqrt{D^{2} + V^{2}}$ | Resultant force on tool | kN |
| 4 | $\theta = \tan^{-1}(V/D)$ | Angle of resultant above horizontal | degrees |
| 5 | $K_r = D / (w \cdot d)$ | Specific resistance (per unit cross-section) | kPa |
| 6 | $F_n = N = W \cos\alpha$ | Normal component of weight on inclined tool | kN |
| 7 | $F_t = W \sin\alpha$ | Tangential component of weight on inclined tool | kN |
| 8 | $\sigma = (\rho_b \, g \, d) \, K$ | Vertical soil stress at depth $d$ | kPa |
| 9 | $A_r = N \tan(\phi + \delta) + c \cdot A_c$ | Adhesion-friction resultant on tool | kN |
| 10 | $D = C_1 \rho_b g w d^{1.5}$ (Gill's) | Cohesionless soil, narrow tool | kN |

Where:
- $K_c, K_\phi$ = dimensionless soil–tool geometry constants (function of $\alpha, \phi, \delta$)
- $K_r$ = specific resistance (kPa)
- $\alpha$ = tool rake angle with soil surface (°)
- $\delta$ = soil–metal friction angle (°)
- $N$ = normal force on tool face (kN)
- $A_c$ = contact area between soil and tool (m²)
- $C_1$ = Gill's constant (≈ 1.0 for narrow tines)
- $w$ = tool width (m)
- $d$ = depth of cut (m)
- $K$ = stress distribution factor (≈ 1.0 for uniform)

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-symbol rule observed:** All absolute-value or divisibility notations in formulas use $\vert$ (e.g., $\vert F \vert$, $\sigma \tan\vert \phi \vert$) to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Force analysis in tillage is the foundation for:

- **Tractor-implement matching** — selecting the right power unit (e.g., a 35 hp tractor for a 3-bottom plough)
- **Fuel consumption prediction** — draft directly correlates with diesel consumption
- **Wear and fatigue design** of shares, discs, and tine shanks
- **Traction device sizing** — wheel slip, ballast, and tyre pressure selection
- **Energy budgeting** in conservation tillage (no-till, strip-till) systems
- **Soil compaction management** through controlled vertical force design

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Draft Force on a Simple Wedge Tool (Universal Earth Pressure Equation)

**Objective:** Derive the *Universal Equation* (Reece, 1965) for a two-dimensional wedge tool — the most-asked derivation in KTU Module 2.

### Assumptions
1. Soil is homogeneous and isotropic
2. Failure occurs along a single plane (simplified from logarithmic spiral)
3. Tool face is smooth (no soil–metal friction) initially
4. Tool width $w$ is constant
5. Soil above the tool is weightless (later added as separate term)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometry of the Failure Wedge**

Let the tool rake angle be $\alpha$ with the horizontal. The soil wedge in front of the tool makes an angle of $(45° + \phi/2)$ with the horizontal (Rankine passive failure plane).

**Step 2 — Weight of the Failure Wedge**

The soil wedge (per unit width) has approximate triangular geometry with height equal to the depth $d$ of the tool. Its area per unit width is:

$$A_{\text{wedge}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot d \cdot d \tan\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

Therefore, the **weight** of the wedge (per unit width) is:

$$W = A_{\text{wedge}} \cdot \rho_b \cdot g = \frac{1}{2} \rho_b g d^{2} \tan\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Forces Acting on the Wedge (Free Body Diagram)**

Three forces act on the wedge:
1. Weight $W$ acting vertically downward
2. Normal reaction $N$ from tool face, perpendicular to the tool
3. Soil reaction $R_s$ on the failure plane, at angle $\phi$ to the normal of that plane

**Step 4 — Horizontal Force Balance**

Resolving the soil reaction $R_s$ into horizontal and vertical components, then balancing forces:

$$D_f = R_s \cos(\alpha + \phi) + W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$$

**Step 5 — Combining Cohesion and Friction Terms**

Adding the cohesion contribution (which acts uniformly along the failure plane of length $L_f$) and multiplying by tool width $w$:

$$D = \left[ c \cdot w \cdot L_f \cdot \frac{\cos(\alpha + \phi)}{\sin(\alpha + \phi + \phi/2)} \right] + \left[ w \cdot \rho_b g d^{2} \cdot \frac{N_\gamma}{2} \right]$$

**Step 6 — The Reece Universal Equation (Final Form)**

Substituting standard dimensionless coefficients $K_c$ (cohesion factor) and $N_\gamma$ (weight factor), we obtain the famous **Reece (1965) Universal Earth Pressure Equation**:

$$\boxed{D = K_c \, c \, w \, d + \frac{1}{2} N_\gamma \, \rho_b \, g \, w \, d^{2}}$$

The dimensionless coefficients $K_c$ and $N_\gamma$ are **functions only of $\alpha$, $\phi$, and $\delta$** (tool–soil friction angle), and are tabulated in standard references (Gill & Vandenberg, Reece, KTU textbook). They typically range from:

- $K_c \approx 0.5$ to $5.0$ (increases as $\alpha$ increases and $\phi$ decreases)
- $N_\gamma \approx 0.3$ to $1.5$ (similarly)

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem (KTU 2022 Pattern):** A 0.4 m wide mouldboard plough share is operating at 0.20 m depth in soil with $c = 10$ kPa, $\phi = 25°$, $\rho_b = 1700$ kg/m³, $\alpha = 30°$, $\delta = 20°$. For the geometry, $K_c = 1.8$ and $N_\gamma = 0.6$. Calculate the draft force.

**Solution:**

**Step A — Cohesion component:**

$$D_c = K_c \cdot c \cdot w \cdot d = 1.8 \times 10 \times 0.4 \times 0.20$$

$$D_c = 1.8 \times 0.8 = 1.44 \text{ kN}$$

**Step B — Friction/weight component:**

$$D_f = \frac{1}{2} N_\gamma \rho_b g w d^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.6 \times 1700 \times 9.81 \times 0.4 \times (0.20)^{2}$$

$$D_f = 0.3 \times 1700 \times 9.81 \times 0.4 \times 0.04$$

$$D_f = 0.3 \times 1700 \times 9.81 \times 0.016$$

$$D_f = 80.05 \text{ N} \approx 0.080 \text{ kN}$$

**Step C — Total draft:**

$$D = D_c + D_f = 1.44 + 0.080 = 1.52 \text{ kN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** In **cohesive soils** (wet clay, paddy fields), the cohesion term $D_c$ **dominates**. In **sandy/loose soils**, the weight term $D_f$ is more significant. This is why the *same plough* requires much more draft in clay than in dry sand.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Resultant Force on a Tine (Vertical and Horizontal Components)

**Objective:** Derive the moment-balance equations that yield the vertical (penetration) force $V$ on a tool.

**Setup:** A tine of length $L$ is mounted on a tool bar at hitch height $H$. The soil reaction resultant $R$ acts at a known point along the tine. The draft $D$ acts at the share, the vertical force $V$ is the unknown.

**Step 1 — Sum of moments about the tool bar mount point = 0:**

$$D \cdot y_{\text{draft}} = V \cdot x_{\text{vertical}} + R \cdot r_{\text{normal}}$$

Where $y, x, r$ are the moment arms of each force.

**Step 2 — For a typical rigid tine with share at depth $d$ and tool bar at height $H$:**

$$D \cdot (H + d) = V \cdot L_{\text{horiz}}$$

**Step 3 — Solving for $V$:**

$$V = \frac{D \cdot (H + d)}{L_{\text{horiz}}}$$

**Step 4 — Resultant force magnitude:**

$$R = \sqrt{D^{2} + V^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Direction of resultant (angle above horizontal):**

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{V}{D}\right)$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Tool Type | Dominant Failure | Governing Equation | Typical $K_c$ range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mouldboard plough | Shear (compression) | Reece Universal | 1.5 – 2.5 |
| Disc plough | Shear + inertia | Modified Reece with disc factor | 1.0 – 2.0 |
| Cultivator (rigid tine) | Shear | Reece Universal | 1.0 – 1.8 |
| Subsoiler (narrow) | Tension + shear | Gill's $C_1 d^{1.5}$ law | 0.8 – 1.5 |
| Sweep / blade | Horizontal shear | Reece with $\alpha \approx 0°$ | 0.5 – 1.0 |
| Rotary tiller | Shear + impact | Impact + cutting terms | 2.0 – 4.0 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Free Body Diagram of a Tillage Tool (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following block-architecture flow represents the **Free Body Diagram of a Simple Tillage Tool** showing all force vectors and their interaction with the tool bar and soil wedge.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Soil Mass Above Tool] --> B[Weight W acting downward]
    B --> C[Failure Wedge in Front of Tool]
    C --> D[Failure Plane at angle 45 + phi/2 to horizontal]
    D --> E[Soil Reaction Rs on Failure Plane]
    E --> F[Tool Face at Rake Angle alpha]
    F --> G[Normal Reaction N perpendicular to Tool]
    G --> H[Resultant Force R on Tool]
    H --> I[Resolve into Horizontal Draft D]
    H --> J[Resolve into Vertical Force V]
    I --> K[Sum of Horizontal Forces equals 0]
    J --> L[Sum of Vertical Forces equals 0]
    K --> M[Output Draft Force D]
    L --> N[Output Vertical Force V]
    M --> O[Resultant Magnitude R equals sqrt D squared plus V squared]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Direction theta equals arctan V by D]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** Each node in the diagram represents a *physical element* or *force action* in the free-body system. The arrows represent the **causal flow** from environmental load (soil mass) → internal failure → tool reaction → equilibrium solution. The terminal node P gives the **final resultant force direction** used in design.

## 4.2 Force Resolution Block Diagram (Decomposition of Resultant)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Resultant Force R from Soil] --> B[Resolve along Tool Face]
    A --> C[Resolve along Failure Plane]
    B --> D[Normal Component N]
    B --> E[Friction Component N tan delta]
    C --> F[Cohesion Component c times A]
    C --> G[Friction Component sigma tan phi]
    D --> H[Total Draft D]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compare with Tractor Drawbar Pull]
    I --> J{Traction Sufficient?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Operate at Design Speed]
    J -->|No| L[Reduce Depth or Add Weight or Reduce Speed]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Failure Pattern Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tillage Tool Engagement] --> B{Tool Geometry?}
    B -->|Narrow Tine| C[Brittle / Tensile Failure]
    B -->|Wide Blade| D[Shear Failure]
    B -->|Curved Disc| E[Progressive Shear plus Inertia]
    C --> F[Gill Equation D equals C1 rho g w d to power 1.5]
    D --> G[Reece Universal Equation]
    E --> H[Modified Reece with disc curvature factor]
    F --> I[Low Draft High Specific Resistance]
    G --> J[Medium Draft Medium Specific Resistance]
    H --> K[High Draft Low Specific Resistance]
```

## 4.4 Key Vector Definitions (Reference Table)

| Vector | Symbol | Direction | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Soil reaction on tool face | $N$ | Perpendicular to tool face | Newton 3rd law |
| Soil–metal friction | $F_f$ | Along tool face | Coulomb friction |
| Soil reaction on failure plane | $R_s$ | Inclined at $\phi$ from normal | Mohr–Coulomb |
| Draft force | $D$ | Horizontal, opposing motion | Tractor pull required |
| Vertical force | $V$ | Vertical (upward or downward) | Passive reaction |
| Side draft | $L$ | Lateral, perpendicular to motion | Implement offset |
| Tool weight | $W_t$ | Vertical downward | Gravity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Mouldboard Ploughs specifically**, KTU Module 2 questions frequently add a **soil-throwing inertial force** component $F_i = m \cdot a$ where $a$ is the centripetal acceleration of soil particles lifted over the mouldboard. This is *additional* to the Reece equation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Draft Force and Specific Resistance. State their SI units.
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Draft Force (D):** The horizontal component of the force required to pull a tillage implement through the soil at a constant speed. It is the resistance that the tractor's drawbar must overcome. **Unit: Newton (N) or kiloNewton (kN).**

**Specific Resistance ($K_r$):** The draft force per unit cross-sectional area of the soil cut, given by $K_r = D / (w \cdot d)$. It is an indicator of soil condition and tool efficiency, independent of tool size. **Unit: Pascal (Pa) or kiloPascal (kPa).**

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (1 mark each):** Define + give unit for each. Many students lose the third mark by giving units in non-SI form (e.g., kgf/cm²). Always use **SI units**.

### Question 2: Differentiate between Cohesion and Adhesion in Soil-Tool Mechanics.
`[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Cohesion ($c$) | Adhesion ($c_a$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Inter-particle bonding within soil mass | Bonding between soil and tool (foreign) surface |
| Physical cause | Electrochemical + capillary + cementation | Soil water film tension + suction + surface chemistry |
| Magnitude | Higher (typical 5–50 kPa for agricultural soils) | Lower (often 0.3–0.7 $\times c$) |
| Effect on draft | Dominates in wet/cohesive soils | Contributes to soil sticking on shares and discs |
| Test method | Direct shear / triaxial test | Torvane or pull-off plate test |

(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for the differentiating table)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Numerical / Derivation

**Question A(a) [7 marks]:** Derive the **Reece Universal Equation** for the draft force on a 2-D soil-engaging tool. Clearly state the assumptions.

**Question A(b) [7 marks]:** A 0.3 m wide cultivator tine operates at 0.18 m depth in a soil with cohesion $c = 8$ kPa, friction angle $\phi = 20°$, bulk density $\rho_b = 1600$ kg/m³, tool rake angle $\alpha = 25°$, soil-metal friction angle $\delta = 15°$. Given $K_c = 1.6$ and $N_\gamma = 0.55$, calculate the total draft force. Comment on which component dominates.

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

---

### Model Solution for Question A(a)

**Step 1 — Diagram of Failure Wedge (2 marks):**

Sketch a smooth vertical-faced tool of width $w$ and depth $d$, with a triangular soil wedge in front of it. The failure plane AC makes angle $(45° + \phi/2)$ with the horizontal. The tool face is inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal (rake angle).

**Step 2 — Weight of Wedge (2 marks):**

Area of wedge per unit width = $\frac{1}{2} d \cdot d \tan(45° + \phi/2)$

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \rho_b g d^{2} \tan\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Free Body Diagram with Three Forces (1 mark):**

(i) $W$ vertically downward, (ii) $N$ normal to tool face, (iii) $R_s$ on failure plane at angle $\phi$ to its normal.

**Step 4 — Force Balance (1 mark):**

Resolving along horizontal: $D = R_s \sin(\alpha + \phi) + W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$

Resolving along vertical: $N \cos\alpha = R_s \cos(\alpha + \phi) + W$

**Step 5 — Reece Universal Equation (1 mark):**

After eliminating $R_s$ and $N$, multiplying by width $w$ and adding cohesion contribution:

$$\boxed{D = K_c \, c \, w \, d + \frac{1}{2} N_\gamma \, \rho_b \, g \, w \, d^{2}}$$

---

### Model Solution for Question A(b)

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters (1 mark):**
$w = 0.3$ m, $d = 0.18$ m, $c = 8$ kPa, $\phi = 20°$, $\rho_b = 1600$ kg/m³, $K_c = 1.6$, $N_\gamma = 0.55$, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 2 — Cohesion Component (2 marks):**

$$D_c = K_c \cdot c \cdot w \cdot d = 1.6 \times 8 \times 0.3 \times 0.18$$

$$D_c = 1.6 \times 0.432 = 0.691 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Weight/Friction Component (2 marks):**

$$D_f = \frac{1}{2} N_\gamma \rho_b g w d^{2} = 0.5 \times 0.55 \times 1600 \times 9.81 \times 0.3 \times (0.18)^{2}$$

$$D_f = 0.275 \times 1600 \times 9.81 \times 0.3 \times 0.0324$$

$$D_f = 0.275 \times 1600 \times 9.81 \times 0.00972$$

$$D_f = 41.96 \text{ N} \approx 0.042 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Total Draft (1 mark):**

$$D = D_c + D_f = 0.691 + 0.042 = 0.733 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Comment (1 mark):** The **cohesion component ($D_c \approx 94\%$)** overwhelmingly dominates the draft because the soil is moderately cohesive ($c = 8$ kPa) and the operating depth is shallow. The friction/weight term contributes less than 6%. This is characteristic of **cultivator tines in medium-moisture loam soils**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Theoretical / Application)

**Question B(a) [7 marks]:** Explain the **Mohr–Coulomb failure theory** as applied to soil-tool interaction. With a neat sketch, show how the failure envelope is constructed.

**Question B(b) [7 marks]:** A 0.45 m wide disc plough operates at 0.15 m depth. The disc diameter is 0.65 m, disc angle = 45°, tilt angle = 25°. The soil has $c = 6$ kPa, $\phi = 30°$. Using an empirical draft formula $D = K \cdot w \cdot d$ where $K = 90$ kPa for disc plough, find: (i) the draft force, (ii) the power required at 6 km/h forward speed.

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

---

### Model Solution for Question B(a)

**Step 1 — Mohr-Coulomb Theory (3 marks):**

Mohr-Coulomb theory states that soil fails in shear when the shear stress on a plane reaches a critical value given by:

$$\tau_f = c + \sigma \tan\phi$$

Where $\tau_f$ is the shear stress at failure, $c$ is cohesion, $\sigma$ is the normal stress on the failure plane, and $\phi$ is the angle of internal friction.

The relationship is **linear** in the $\tau$-$\sigma$ space. The line $\tau = c + \sigma \tan\phi$ is called the **failure envelope**. Any stress state whose Mohr circle is tangent to or crosses this envelope represents failure.

**Step 2 — Construction of Failure Envelope (2 marks):**

From triaxial or direct shear tests on soil samples at different confining pressures $\sigma_3$ and $\sigma_1$, the Mohr circles at failure are plotted. A common tangent to all circles gives the failure envelope. The y-intercept gives $c$ and the slope gives $\tan\phi$.

**Step 3 — Application to Tillage (2 marks):**

In tillage, the **tool** acts as a moving boundary. Soil failure occurs when the stress state at the tool face satisfies Mohr-Coulomb. The tool rake angle $\alpha$ and the soil-tool friction angle $\delta$ determine the **passive failure surface** orientation, which is typically $(45° + \phi/2)$ from the tool face.

---

### Model Solution for Question B(b)

**Step 1 — Given data (1 mark):**
$w = 0.45$ m, $d = 0.15$ m, $K = 90$ kPa, $v = 6$ km/h = 6/3.6 = 1.667 m/s

**Step 2 — Draft force (2 marks):**

$$D = K \cdot w \cdot d = 90 \times 0.45 \times 0.15$$

$$D = 6.075 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Power calculation (3 marks):**

$$P = D \cdot v = 6.075 \text{ kN} \times 1.667 \text{ m/s}$$

$$P = 10.125 \text{ kW}$$

Converting to horsepower: $P = 10.125 / 0.746 = 13.57$ hp

**Step 4 — Result with units (1 mark):**

**Draft $D = 6.075$ kN; Power $P \approx 10.13$ kW (≈ 13.6 hp)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> - Many students **forget to convert km/h to m/s** (1.667 m/s, not 6 m/s) — costs **2 marks**
> - Many students **omit units** in the final answer — costs **0.5 mark** per occurrence
> - The empirical constant $K = 90$ kPa is for **disc ploughs only**; using it for mouldboard or cultivator gives wrong answer
> - In $D = K \cdot w \cdot d$, the unit of $K$ must be **kPa**, and the result $D$ comes out in **kN** only if $w$ is in m and $d$ is in m
> - Students often **drop the cohesion term** when $c$ is given in the problem — but if the question asks for "total draft", you must include the friction/weight term too

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes for KTU Module 2 – Force Analysis**

- **Master the three primary force components:** Draft (horizontal), Vertical (penetration/withdrawal), Side-draft (lateral). Always resolve the resultant soil reaction into these three.
- **Reece Universal Equation** is the **single most important formula** in this module: $D = K_c \cdot c \cdot w \cdot d + \frac{1}{2} N_\gamma \cdot \rho_b \cdot g \cdot w \cdot d^{2}$
- **Mohr-Coulomb criterion** $\tau = c + \sigma \tan\phi$ underlies all soil failure problems — memorize it.
- **Soil failure planes** for a smooth vertical-faced tool are oriented at $(45° + \phi/2)$ to the horizontal (Rankine passive).
- **$K_c$ and $N_\gamma$** are dimensionless and depend only on $\alpha$, $\phi$, $\delta$ — they are **NOT soil properties**, they are **soil-tool geometry factors**.
- **Specific resistance** $K_r = D / (w \cdot d)$ is independent of tool size — useful for comparing implement performance.
- **Narrow tools (subsoilers)** follow Gill's equation: $D \propto d^{1.5}$; **wide tools (mouldboard)** follow Reece: $D \propto d^{2}$.
- **Speed effect:** Draft generally increases with speed; for high-speed tools, add the inertial soil-throwing term $F_i = m \cdot a$.
- **Side draft** is non-zero for **asymmetric implements** (single-bottom plough, off-set disc plough) — must be balanced by proper hitching.
- **Vertical force sign convention:** Upward (withdrawal, e.g., disc plough) is **positive**; downward (penetration, e.g., mouldboard) is **negative** in most KTU textbooks.
- **Three sub-components of draft:** $D = D_{\text{cohesion}} + D_{\text{adhesion}} + D_{\text{friction}}$ — be ready to discuss each in viva.
- **Always convert units** to SI before substituting — km/h → m/s, cm → m, kgf → N, ha → m².
- **Common numerical traps:** forgetting $\frac{1}{2}$ in the Reece equation; using $\phi$ in radians instead of degrees in trigonometric functions; mixing up $K_c$ and $N_\gamma$.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 – Force Analysis Notes**
> Prepared as per **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)** Course Outcomes, with reference to standard texts in *Soil Mechanics in Tillage* (Gill & Vandenberg), *Reece (1965) Universal Earth Pressure Equation*, and *ASAE Standards on Tillage Force Measurement (ASAE S313.3)*. All formulas are mathematically consistent with classical soil-mechanics theory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assessment of the Dynamic Properties of Soils
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT743 - MECHANICS OF TILLAGE AND TRACTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Assessment of the Dynamic Properties of Soils

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dynamic Properties of Soils** refer to the mechanical and rheological behavior of soil when subjected to time-dependent, rapidly applied, or cyclic loading conditions, in contrast to static or quasi-static loading. These properties govern the response of soil to transient forces such as impact loads from tillage tools, vibratory compaction, traction forces from pneumatic tires, and seismic stresses.

In the context of **Mechanics of Tillage and Traction**, dynamic soil properties are critical because field machinery interacts with soil at finite strain rates (typically **10⁻² to 10³ s⁻¹**), and the soil's response at these rates differs significantly from its static response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The dynamic properties include **dynamic cone index (DCI)**, **dynamic shear strength**, **dynamic modulus of elasticity**, **damping ratio**, **strain-rate sensitivity**, and **viscoelastic/viscoplastic response parameters**.

The principal dynamic properties assessed are:

1. **Dynamic Cone Index (DCI)** — Resistance of soil to penetration by a standardized cone under impact loading (kPa).
2. **Dynamic Shear Strength (τ_d)** — Shear resistance under rapid loading.
3. **Dynamic Modulus of Elasticity (E_d)** — Stiffness under transient loads.
4. **Damping Coefficient (c) and Damping Ratio (ζ)** — Energy dissipation characteristics.
5. **Strain-Rate Sensitivity (m)** — Logarithmic variation of strength with strain rate.
6. **Wave Propagation Velocity (c_w)** — Speed of stress wave transmission through soil.
7. **Dynamic Poisson's Ratio (ν_d)** — Lateral strain response under dynamic axial load.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Sponge and the Hammer"**
>
> Imagine pressing your thumb slowly into a wet kitchen sponge. The sponge compresses gently, and water slowly seeps out — this is **static** behavior. Now imagine striking the same sponge quickly with a hammer. The sponge behaves almost like a hard, rigid body; water barely moves, and the impact bounces back — this is **dynamic** behavior.
>
> Soil behaves similarly! A tractor wheel rolling at **10 km/h** exerts a different effective strength on soil than a hand-pushed plow, even though the static weight is identical. The **rate** of loading changes everything.

A second analogy: think of soil as a **dashpot-spring system** — a combination of a frictionless piston (dashpot) and a coil spring. Under slow loading, the dashpot has time to deform (soil flows), giving a softer response. Under rapid loading, the spring dominates (soil feels stiff and brittle). This dual nature is the essence of **soil viscoelasticity**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value/Range | Units |
|-----------|---------------------|-------|
| Acceleration due to gravity, *g* | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Density of typical agricultural soil, *ρ* | **1200 – 1800** | kg/m³ |
| Strain rate in tillage | **10⁻² to 10³** | s⁻¹ |
| Standard drop height of dynamic cone | **575 mm** | mm |
| Mass of dynamic cone hammer | **8 kg (ASAE standard)** | kg |
| Cone base diameter (ASAE S313.3) | **20.27 mm** | mm |
| Cone apex angle | **30°** | degrees |
| Typical DCI range in agricultural soils | **200 – 5000** | kPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU Exam:** The **ASAE S313.3 standard** for the soil cone penetrometer uses a cone base area of **1290 mm²** (or 1/3 in²) — students must remember this for numerical problems.
