# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set A]**

**Q1. (3 marks)** Define the **Bekker pressure–sinkage equation** and explain the physical significance of the terms $k_c$, $k_\phi$, and $n$.

**Model Answer:**

> The Bekker pressure–sinkage equation (1960) gives the relationship between the pressure $p$ applied to a flat plate and the resulting sinkage $z$ in the soil:
> $$p = \left(\dfrac{k_c}{b} + k_\phi\right) z^n$$
> where:
> - $k_c$ = **cohesive modulus of soil** in $\text{kN/m}^{(n+1)}$ — represents the soil's resistance due to **cohesion** (clay-like behaviour). The $1/b$ term makes it scale with contact **area**.
> - $k_\phi$ = **frictional modulus of soil** in $\text{kN/m}^{(n+2)}$ — represents the soil's resistance due to **internal friction** (sand-like behaviour). It scales with **volume** of displaced soil.
> - $n$ = **sinkage exponent**, dimensionless — describes the **non-linearity** of the pressure-sinkage curve. Typical values: $n = 0.5$ (sands) to $n = 1.5$ (stiff clays); for most agricultural soils $n \approx 1$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]**

**Q2. (3 marks)** What is the **Wong wheel number $C_n$**? Why is it called a "dimensionless" number?

**Model Answer:**

> Wong's wheel number (1979) is defined as:
> $$C_n = \dfrac{CI \cdot b \cdot d}{W}$$
> where $CI$ = cone index (kPa), $b$ = wheel width (m), $d$ = wheel diameter (m), $W$ = wheel load (kN).
> **Dimensional check:**
> - $CI$ has units of $\text{kN/m}^2$
> - $b \cdot d$ has units of $\text{m}^2$
> - $W$ has units of $\text{kN}$
> - Therefore, $C_n$ has units of $\dfrac{\text{kN/m}^2 \cdot \text{m}^2}{\text{kN}} = 1$ (dimensionless).
>
> It is **dimensionless** because it is a ratio that consolidates **soil stiffness** (numerator) with **wheel load intensity** (denominator). A small $C_n$ indicates soft soil relative to the wheel load, while a large $C_n$ indicates firm soil.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice A or B)

> **[KTU University Exam – December 2024 Model Paper, Module 3]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**A(a)** (7 marks) Derive the **Bekker pressure–sinkage equation** starting from the assumption that the soil pressure varies as a power function of sinkage. Clearly explain the role of each parameter.

**A(b)** (7 marks) A tractor's pneumatic tyre has width $b = 0.30$ m, diameter $d = 1.10$ m, and supports a vertical load $W = 10$ kN on a soil with cone index $CI = 1500$ kPa. Using Wong's equations, calculate the **net traction ratio** and the **motion resistance ratio** at 20% slip. Hence determine the **tractive efficiency**.

---

### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Step 1 — Hypothesis:**
Bekker assumed that for a flat plate of width $b$ pushed into a soil mass, the pressure $p$ required is a power function of the sinkage $z$:

$$
p = K_0 \cdot z^n
$$

where $K_0$ is a proportionality constant depending on soil and $n$ is a dimensionless sinkage exponent.

**Step 2 — Decomposition of soil moduli:**
Bekker proposed that $K_0$ can be split into two physical components:

$$
p = \left(\frac{k_c}{b} + k_\phi\right) z^n
$$

where:
- $k_c$ is the **cohesive modulus** (representing clay-like resistance, scales with area)
- $k_\phi$ is the **frictional modulus** (representing sand-like resistance, scales with volume)
- $b$ is the contact width

**Step 3 — Justification of form:**
The $1/b$ factor on $k_c$ ensures dimensional consistency: $[k_c] = \text{kN/m}^{(n+1)}$, while $[k_\phi] = \text{kN/m}^{(n+2)}$.

**Step 4 — Determination of $k_c, k_\phi, n$ via Bevameter test:**
Bekker designed a **plate penetrometer test** (Bevameter) with two plate sizes (long, narrow) to obtain $k_c, k_\phi, n$ from regression of measured $(z, p)$ pairs in the logarithmic domain:

$$
\log(p) = \log\left(\frac{k_c}{b} + k_\phi\right) + n \cdot \log(z)
$$

**Step 5 — Physical meaning:**
The equation captures two distinct failure regimes in soil:
- **Cohesive failure** (shear surfaces) — represented by $k_c$
- **Frictional failure** (inter-particle sliding) — represented by $k_\phi$

This is the foundational equation for all subsequent traction and compaction models.

---

### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Step 1 — Compute Wong's wheel number $C_n$:**

$$
C_n = \frac{CI \cdot b \cdot d}{W} = \frac{1500 \times 0.30 \times 1.10}{10} = \frac{495}{10} = 49.5
$$

Since $C_n = 49.5 > 5$, Wong's high-$C_n$ equations are valid.

**Step 2 — Net traction ratio at 20% slip:**

$$
\frac{H}{W} = 0.796 - \frac{0.92}{C_n} = 0.796 - \frac{0.92}{49.5} = 0.796 - 0.01859 = 0.7774
$$

Therefore, **net traction** $H = 0.7774 \times 10 = 7.774$ kN

**Step 3 — Motion resistance ratio at 20% slip:**

$$
\frac{\rho}{W} = \frac{0.49}{\sqrt{C_n}} = \frac{0.49}{\sqrt{49.5}} = \frac{0.49}{7.0356} = 0.06965
$$

Therefore, **motion resistance** $\rho = 0.06965 \times 10 = 0.6965$ kN

**Step 4 — Tractive efficiency:**

$$
\eta_t = \frac{(1-i)H - \rho \cdot i}{(H + \rho) \cdot i}
$$

Wait — let us use the **standard form** of tractive efficiency:

$$
\eta_t = \frac{P \cdot (1-i) - \rho}{(P + \rho) \cdot i}
$$

where $P = H$ (net traction). With $i = 0.20$:

$$
\eta_t = \frac{7.774 \times (1 - 0.20) - 0.6965}{(7.774 + 0.6965) \times 0.20}
$$

$$
\eta_t = \frac{7.774 \times 0.80 - 0.6965}{8.4705 \times 0.20} = \frac{6.219 - 0.6965}{1.6941}
$$

$$
\eta_t = \frac{5.5225}{1.6941} = 0.326 \text{ or } 32.6\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** A tractive efficiency of ~33% is reasonable for a soft-soil operation at 20% slip. Efficiency rises significantly when slip is reduced (e.g., 10% slip gives ~55% efficiency).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**B(a)** (7 marks) Explain the **Mohr–Coulomb failure criterion** and the **Janosi–Hanamoto shear stress–displacement equation**. How are they combined to predict wheel traction?

**B(b)** (7 marks) A 3-bottom mouldboard plough has tillage depth $T = 22$ cm and width per body $W = 0.35$ m. The ASAE coefficients are $A = 240$, $B = 95$, $C = 14$ (with $S$ in km/h). The soil texture is **fine** ($F_i = 1.0$) and the operating speed is $S = 7$ km/h. Calculate the **total draft** of the plough.

---

### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**Step 1 — Mohr–Coulomb Failure Criterion:**
The Mohr–Coulomb equation gives the maximum shear stress that a soil can sustain at a given normal stress $\sigma$:

$$
\tau_{max} = c + \sigma \tan\phi
$$

where $c$ = cohesion, $\phi$ = internal friction angle. This is the **failure envelope**; the soil cannot sustain shear stress beyond this.

**Step 2 — Janosi–Hanamoto Equation:**
When a track or wheel shears the soil, the soil's resistance rises **exponentially** with shear displacement $j$:

$$
\tau = \tau_{max}\left(1 - e^{-j/K}\right)
$$

- At $j = 0$: $\tau = 0$
- At $j \to \infty$: $\tau \to \tau_{max}$
- $K$ is the **shear deformation modulus** (cm)

**Step 3 — Combination for Wheel Traction:**

The **net traction** $H$ on a powered wheel is obtained by integrating $\tau$ over the contact patch length $l$:

$$
H = b \cdot \int_0^{l} \tau(x)\, dx
$$

Substituting Janosi–Hanamoto's equation and using the slip-dependent shear displacement $j(x) = j_{max}(1 - x/l)$:

$$
H = b \cdot \tau_{max} \int_0^{l} \left[1 - e^{-j_{max}(1 - x/l)/K}\right] dx
$$

With the Mohr–Coulomb value of $\tau_{max}$ and the wheel-load-dependent $j_{max}$ from Bekker's sinkage, this integral is **numerically evaluated** to yield $H$ as a function of $W$, $i$, $b$, $d$, and soil properties. Wong (1979) then **curve-fitted** the numerical results to obtain the empirical equations (P6, P7).

---

### Model Solution — Question B(b)

Given:
- $T = 22$ cm, $W = 0.35$ m, $F_i = 1.0$, $S = 7$ km/h
- $A = 240$, $B = 95$, $C = 14$

**Step 1 — Draft per body:**

$$
D_{body} = F_i \cdot \left[A + B \cdot S + C \cdot S^2\right] \cdot T \cdot W
$$

$$
D_{body} = 1.0 \times \left[240 + 95 \times 7 + 14 \times 49\right] \times 22 \times 0.35
$$

$$
D_{body} = \left[240 + 665 + 686\right] \times 7.7
$$

$$
D_{body} = 1591 \times 7.7 = 12250.7 \text{ N} \approx 12.25 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Total draft:**

$$
D_{total} = n_b \times D_{body} = 3 \times 12.25 = 36.76 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** A 3-bottom plough at 7 km/h on fine soil at 22 cm depth should require approximately **30 – 40 kN**. Result: 36.76 kN ✔.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** A frequent mistake is to mix units of speed (km/h with m/s coefficients) — always check whether ASAE coefficients use km/h or m/s.
> 2. **Cone index units:** Some texts use kPa, others use MPa. **1 MPa = 1000 kPa**. Mixing them leads to $C_n$ errors of 1000×.
> 3. **Bekker sign convention:** $p$ and $z$ are always **positive**; do not introduce negative signs.
> 4. **Slip ratio definition:** Some texts use $i = (V_t - V_a)/V_t$, others $i = (V_t - V_a)/V_a$. KTU follows the **theoretical-velocity form** (P11).
> 5. **High $C_n$ assumption:** Wong's P6 and P7 are valid **only for $C_n > 5$**. For $C_n < 5$ (very soft soil), use Bekker-based or other low-$C_n$ branches. Failure to check this loses **2 marks** typically.
> 6. **Failing to box the final answer:** In KTU 2024, **unboxed final answers** may lose **0.5 mark** per sub-part. Always use $\boxed{}$ or draw a box around the final numerical result.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Development of Prediction Equations**

### Core Equations to Memorize

- [ ] **Bekker P1:** $p = \left(\dfrac{k_c}{b} + k_\phi\right) z^n$ — pressure–sinkage
- [ ] **Janosi–Hanamoto P3:** $\tau = \tau_{max}\left(1 - e^{-j/K}\right)$ — shear–displacement
- [ ] **Mohr–Coulomb P4:** $\tau_{max} = c + \sigma \tan\phi$ — failure envelope
- [ ] **Wong P5:** $C_n = \dfrac{CI \cdot b \cdot d}{W}$ — dimensionless wheel number
- [ ] **Wong P6:** $\dfrac{H}{W} = 0.796 - \dfrac{0.92}{C_n}$ at $i = 0.20$
- [ ] **Wong P7:** $\dfrac{\rho}{W} = \dfrac{0.49}{\sqrt{C_n}}$ at $i = 0.20$
- [ ] **P8:** $\eta_t = \dfrac{P(1-i) - \rho}{(P+\rho)\, i}$ — tractive efficiency
- [ ] **ASAE P9:** $D = F_i \cdot \left[A + B \cdot S + C \cdot S^2\right] \cdot T \cdot W$
- [ ] **Slip P11:** $i = 1 - V_{actual}/V_{theoretical}$
- [ ] **Cone Index P13:** $CI = P_{cone}/A_{cone}$ with $A_{cone} = 323 \text{ mm}^2$

### Numerical Value Anchors

- [ ] ASAE standard cone base area = **323 mm²** (= 1.3 cm²)
- [ ] Standard soil moisture for tillage tests = field capacity (~25–30% w/w for loam)
- [ ] Typical $n$ for agricultural soils = **0.7 – 1.3**
- [ ] Wong equations valid for $C_n > 5$ (high-soil-strength regime)
- [ ] Bekker Bevameter plate widths typically: 50 mm and 200 mm

### Conceptual Anchors

- [ ] Bekker's **two-moduli hypothesis** is the foundation — always mention it
- [ ] Wong's equations are **empirical fits to integrated soil mechanics** — not pure theory
- [ ] $C_n$ is essentially the **soil-stiffness-to-wheel-load ratio**
- [ ] ASAE draft equation is **dimensioned**, while Wong is **dimensionless** — do not confuse
- [ ] Perumpral's model is **fully empirical** and tyre-specific — used for precision farming
- [ ] Bekker→Janosi–Hanamoto→Wong is the **chain of integration**: pressure → shear → traction

### Common Examination Traps to Avoid

- [ ] Always check the **slip value** at which Wong's P6 and P7 are valid (i = 20%)
- [ ] For $i \neq 20\%$, use the **general Wong charts** or recompute via Perumpral
- [ ] Don't forget to **multiply by $n_b$** for multi-body implements
- [ ] Don't confuse **soil texture factor $F_i$** values (Fine = 1.0, Medium = 0.88, Coarse = 0.78)
- [ ] Always write **units** in the final answer (kN, m, etc.)

### One-Line Takeaway

> [!NOTE]
> **Prediction equations convert soil "fingerprints" (CI, c, φ, kc, kφ, n) into machine "behaviour" (draft, slip, traction, efficiency) — they are the engineer's shortcut to designing tillage systems without running a new field experiment for every soil type.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Traction and Mechanics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT743 - MECHANICS OF TILLAGE AND TRACTION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Traction and Mechanics # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Traction In the context of **off-road mobility** and **agricultural engineering**, **traction** is defined as the sustained frictional interaction between a powered traction device (typically a pneumatic tyre, track, or cage wheel) and the deformable soil surface, which generates a usable horizontal propulsive force to overcome motion resistance and pull an external load. According to the standard KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature for PEAGT743: - **Tractive Effort (Tractive Force):** The total horizontal force developed at the contact patch of the traction device against the soil. - **Net Traction:** The portion of tractive effort that is available to perform useful work (i.e., pulling an implement). - **Tractive Efficiency:** The ratio of useful work output at the drawbar to the total work input at the wheel-soil interface. Mathematically, the most fundamental relationship in traction mechanics is: $$T_{net} = T_{gross} - R$$ where $T_{net}$ is the **net traction (N)**, $T_{gross}$ is the **gross traction (N)**, and $R$ is the **total motion resistance (N)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition to Memorise:** Traction is the process of developing a horizontal pull from a powered wheel or track against a soil surface, such that the soil reaction at the contact patch provides the propulsive force required for vehicle locomotion and load pulling. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are wearing rubber-soled shoes and trying to walk forward on a wet, muddy farm field. To move ahead, you must **push backward against the mud** with your foot. The mud pushes back on you (Newton's Third Law), and that reaction is what propels your body forward. - The **backward push** you apply = the torque-driven shear stress at the contact patch. - The **mud's reaction on you** = the Gross Traction. - The **energy lost in deforming mud, sinking, and slipping** = Motion Resistance. - The **net force that actually moves your body forward** = Net Traction. Now imagine you are also dragging a heavy log behind you. Some of that mud-reaction force is "spent" just pulling the log — that portion becomes the **Drawbar Pull** available for the implement. > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** Soil is not a rigid surface like a road. It deforms, shears, and compacts. Therefore, traction on soil is fundamentally a **soil mechanics problem**, not a friction-only problem. A KTU examiner will always expect you to link traction to **soil shear strength** and **wheel-soil contact pressure distribution**. ## 1.3 Standardised KTU Symbols & Units | Symbol | Quantity | Unit (SI) | |--------|----------|-----------| | $T_g$ | Gross traction | **N** | | $T_n$ | Net traction | **N** | | $R$ | Motion resistance | **N** | | $W$ | Dynamic wheel load | **N** | | $P$ | Inflation pressure | **kPa** | | $S$ | Wheel slip | **% (decimal)** | | $\mu$ | Coefficient of traction | **dimensionless** | | $\rho$ | Motion resistance ratio | **dimensionless** | | $V_t$ | Theoretical forward speed | **m/s** | | $V_a$ | Actual forward speed | **m/s** | | $A$ | Contact patch area | **m²** | | $C_n$ | Wheel numeric (cone index ratio) | **dimensionless** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Free Body Analysis of a Powered Wheel on Soft Soil When a powered (driven) wheel operates on deformable soil, **four** distinct forces and reactions act on the wheel: 1. **Vertical Dynamic Load ($W$):** The load carried by the wheel, applied at the wheel centre. 2. **Gross Traction Force ($T_g$):** The horizontal soil-reaction force generated at the contact patch due to the driving torque applied at the axle. 3. **Motion Resistance ($R$):** The horizontal resistance to forward motion, arising from soil compaction (rolling resistance) and tyre flexure. 4. **Wheel Pull (Drawbar Force, $DP$):** The external horizontal force used to pull an implement. ### 2.2 Logical Flow of Force Generation - The engine transmits a **driving torque** ($M$) to the wheel axle. - The tyre tread attempts to shear the soil **backwards** at the contact patch. - By **Newton's Third Law**, the soil exerts an equal and opposite **forward** reaction on the wheel = $T_g$. - However, as the wheel rolls forward, it must continually **compact fresh soil** and overcome **internal tyre hysteresis** losses. This dissipative force is $R$. - The **remaining** horizontal force is what can be used to pull a load = $T_n = T_g - R$. - When an implement is hitched, the **drawbar pull** $DP \leq T_n$ (the difference accounts for any additional implement-induced resistance during motion). ## 2.3 The Traction Coefficient (Coefficient of Net Traction) The **coefficient of traction** ($\mu$) is the most important dimensionless index in off-road vehicle design. It is defined as: $$\mu = \frac{T_{net}}{W} = \frac{T_{gross} - R}{W}$$ This is also written as: $$\mu = \mu_{gross} - \rho$$ where: - $\mu_{gross} = T_g / W$ is the **gross traction coefficient**. - $\rho = R / W$ is the **motion resistance ratio**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** The coefficient of traction is **NOT** a constant. It is a function of **slip**, **soil type**, **soil moisture**, **wheel load**, **tyre size**, and **inflation pressure**. The relationship $\mu = f(S)$ is the central design chart in traction mechanics (the Brixius / Wismer-Luth model). ## 2.4 Wheel Slip — The "Loss" in Traction Because the soil deforms, a driven wheel never travels its theoretical distance. The wheel "slips" relative to the ground. Slip is defined as: $$S = \frac{V_t - V_a}{V_t} = 1 - \frac{V_a}{V_t}$$ - $V_t$ = theoretical speed = $\omega \cdot r$ (angular velocity $\times$ rolling radius). - $V_a$ = actual ground speed measured over a test run. > [!NOTE] > **Slip of 0%** means the wheel is rolling freely with no soil deformation (rare on soft soil). > **Slip of 100%** means the wheel is spinning in place — full energy dissipation, zero useful traction. > **Typical optimum operating slip** for 2WD tractors on firm soil is **10–15%**. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Equation | Description | Typical Range / Unit | |---|----------|-------------|----------------------| | 1 | $T_n = T_g - R$ | Net traction balance | **N** | | 2 | $\mu = T_n / W$ | Coefficient of net traction | **0.0 – 0.6** | | 3 | $\mu_g = T_g / W$ | Gross traction coefficient | **0.1 – 0.9** | | 4 | $\rho = R / W$ | Motion resistance ratio | **0.05 – 0.40** | | 5 | $S = (V_t - V_a)/V_t$ | Wheel slip (decimal) | **0 – 1** | | 6 | $DP = T_n - R_{hitch}$ | Drawbar pull | **N** | | 7 | $W = W_{static} + W_{trans}$ | Total dynamic axle load | **N** | | 8 | $T_{pull} \propto (c \cdot A + W \tan\phi)$ | Bekker-style soil reaction | **N** | | 9 | $P_{contact} = W / A$ | Mean contact pressure | **kPa** | | 10 | $\eta_t = (DP \cdot V_a) / (T_g \cdot V_t)$ | Tractive efficiency | **0 – 1** | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The traction mechanics framework is the foundation for: - **Tractor-implement matching** (selecting the right ballast, tyre pressure, and gear). - **4WD optimisation** for paddy, sandy, and clayey soils. - **Off-road military and mining vehicle design**. - **Fuel efficiency modelling** (excess slip wastes fuel as heat in soil). - **Soil compaction prediction** (Wong-Reece equation, Bekker theory). - **Autonomous agricultural robot path planning** on soft terrain. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Net Traction Equation ### Given System: A driven pneumatic tyre of radius $r$ (m) is mounted on a tractor axle carrying a vertical dynamic load $W$ (N). A torque $M$ (N·m) is applied at the axle. The tyre interacts with a soft agricultural soil. ### Step 1 — Force Balance on the Wheel in the Horizontal Direction Consider the wheel as a free body. The horizontal forces acting on it are: - $T_g$ acting **forward** at the contact patch (soil reaction). - $R$ acting **backward** at the contact patch (motion resistance). - $DP$ acting **backward** at the hitch (drawbar pull, when load is attached). For a **no-load** condition (wheel rolling freely under torque, no implement attached), $DP = 0$. Force balance gives: $$T_g - R - 0 = M_{net} \cdot a$$ where $a$ is the linear acceleration. At **steady state** ($a = 0$): $$T_g = R$$ This is the **no-load rolling condition** — all gross traction is consumed by motion resistance. ### Step 2 — Introduction of External Load When a drawbar load $DP$ is applied, the steady-state horizontal force balance becomes: $$T_g - R - DP = 0$$ Rearranging: $$T_g = R + DP$$ The **net traction** is defined as the portion of gross traction that is available to overcome external load (i.e., what remains after paying for motion resistance): $$T_n \equiv T_g - R$$ Substituting the rearranged force balance: $$T_n = T_g - R = DP$$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical Insight:** Under steady-state rolling, the **net traction numerically equals the drawbar pull**. This is the bedrock identity used in KTU field-test problems and ASAE standard S296.5 (drawbar pull test). ### Step 3 — Definition of Coefficient of Traction Dividing both sides of the net traction equation by the dynamic wheel load $W$: $$\frac{T_n}{W} = \frac{T_g}{W} - \frac{R}{W}$$ Defining: $$\mu \equiv \frac{T_n}{W}, \quad \mu_g \equiv \frac{T_g}{W}, \quad \rho \equiv \frac{R}{W}$$ we obtain the elegant **traction coefficient decomposition**: $$\mu = \mu_g - \rho$$ This is the single most-tested equation in KTU Module 4 derivations. ### Step 4 — Derivation of Slip Consider a wheel of rolling radius $r$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s). - **Theoretical forward speed** (no slip): $V_t = \omega \cdot r$ - **Actual forward speed** (with slip): $V_a$ (measured) The "lost" distance per revolution is: $$\Delta d = V_t \cdot T - V_a \cdot T = (V_t - V_a) \cdot T$$ where $T$ is the period of one revolution. The **fractional slip** is: $$S = \frac{\Delta d}{V_t \cdot T} = \frac{V_t - V_a}{V_t} = 1 - \frac{V_a}{V_t}$$ In percentage form: $$S(\%) = \left(1 - \frac{V_a}{V_t}\right) \times 100$$ ### Step 5 — Tractive Efficiency Derivation Tractive efficiency $\eta_t$ is defined as the ratio of useful power output (at the drawbar) to power input (at the wheel-soil contact): $$\eta_t = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{DP \cdot V_a}{T_g \cdot V_t}$$ From Step 2, under steady state, $DP = T_g - R$. Also, by definition of slip, $V_a = V_t (1 - S)$. Substituting: $$\eta_t = \frac{(T_g - R) \cdot V_t(1 - S)}{T_g \cdot V_t}$$ $$\eta_t = \frac{T_g - R}{T_g} \cdot (1 - S)$$ $$\eta_t = \left(1 - \frac{R}{T_g}\right)(1 - S)$$ Or, equivalently, using coefficients: $$\eta_t = \left(1 - \frac{\rho}{\mu_g}\right)(1 - S)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**. The peak tractive efficiency typically occurs at slip $S \approx 10$–$15\%$ for agricultural tractor tyres on firm soil, and at $S \approx 20$–$30\%$ on loose sand. ## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example **Problem:** A 2WD tractor pulls an implement on a tilled loam soil. The following data are recorded during a KTU-style field test: - Dynamic load on each drive wheel: $W = 9{,}000$ N - Gross traction at each drive wheel: $T_g = 7{,}200$ N - Motion resistance at each drive wheel: $R = 2{,}700$ N - Theoretical speed (no slip): $V_t = 2.5$ m/s - Actual measured speed: $V_a = 2.1$ m/s **Find:** (a) Net traction per wheel, (b) Coefficient of traction, (c) Motion resistance ratio, (d) Gross traction coefficient, (e) Wheel slip, (f) Drawbar pull per wheel, (g) Tractive efficiency. ### Solution (Step-by-Step): **(a) Net traction per wheel** $$T_n = T_g - R = 7{,}200 - 2{,}700 = 4{,}500 \text{ N}$$ **(b) Coefficient of traction** $$\mu = \frac{T_n}{W} = \frac{4{,}500}{9{,}000} = 0.50$$ **(c) Motion resistance ratio** $$\rho = \frac{R}{W} = \frac{2{,}700}{9{,}000} = 0.30$$ **(d) Gross traction coefficient** $$\mu_g = \frac{T_g}{W} = \frac{7{,}200}{9{,}000} = 0.80$$ **Verification using the decomposition equation:** $$\mu = \mu_g - \rho = 0.80 - 0.30 = 0.50 \quad \checkmark$$ **(e) Wheel slip** $$S = \frac{V_t - V_a}{V_t} = \frac{2.5 - 2.1}{2.5} = 0.16 = 16\%$$ **(f) Drawbar pull per wheel** Under steady state: $$DP = T_n = 4{,}500 \text{ N}$$ For the two-wheel tractor: $$DP_{total} = 2 \times 4{,}500 = 9{,}000 \text{ N}$$ **(g) Tractive efficiency** $$\eta_t = \left(1 - \frac{\rho}{\mu_g}\right)(1 - S)$$ $$\eta_t = \left(1 - \frac{0.30}{0.80}\right)(1 - 0.16)$$ $$\eta_t = (1 - 0.375)(0.84) = 0.625 \times 0.84 = 0.525 = 52.5\%$$ **Or via direct power ratio:** $$\eta_t = \frac{DP \cdot V_a}{T_g \cdot V_t} = \frac{4{,}500 \times 2.1}{7{,}200 \times 2.5} = \frac{9{,}450}{18{,}000} = 0.525 \quad \checkmark$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Free Body Diagram of a Driven Wheel on Soil Since Mermaid cannot render physical engineering drawings with realistic tyre deformation, the diagram below presents a **schematic free-body representation** with all forces and reactions labelled exactly as required in KTU board examination answers. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph WHEEL["DRIVEN WHEEL (Free Body)"] DIR["Direction of forward motion: -->"] end W["W = Dynamic vertical load (N)
acts DOWN at axle"] TG["T_g = Gross traction (N)
acts FORWARD at contact patch"] R["R = Motion resistance (N)
acts BACKWARD at contact patch"] DP["DP = Drawbar pull (N)
acts BACKWARD at hitch point"] M["M = Driving torque (N.m)
acts CW at axle"] CP["Contact patch with soil
(shear zone)"] W --> WHEEL TG --> WHEEL R --> WHEEL DP --> WHEEL M --> WHEEL WHEEL -.-> CP ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Energy Flow in the Traction System ```mermaid flowchart LR A["ENGINE
(Chemical energy)"] --> B["TRANSMISSION
(Torque M)"] B --> C["WHEEL-SOIL INTERFACE
(Contact patch)"] C --> D["GROSS TRACTION T_g
(Soil shear reaction)"] D --> E["MOTION RESISTANCE R
(Soil compaction + tyre losses)"] D --> F["NET TRACTION T_n"] F --> G["DRAWBAR PULL DP
(Useful work)"] G --> H["IMPLEMENT
(Field operation)"] E --> I["WASTED HEAT
(in soil and tyre)"] F --> I ``` ## 4.3 Traction Coefficient Decomposition Chart (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart TB MU["Coefficient of Traction mu = T_n / W"] MUG["Gross Traction Coefficient
mu_g = T_g / W"] RHO["Motion Resistance Ratio
rho = R / W"] EQUATION["mu = mu_g - rho"] MUG --> EQUATION RHO --> EQUATION EQUATION --> MU ``` ## 4.4 Slip vs. Traction Performance Curve (Block Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["S = 0%
No slip
mu approx 0
Pure rolling"] --> S2["S = 5-10%
mu rising steeply
Optimal zone"] S2 --> S3["S = 15-20%
mu approx max
Peak efficiency"] S3 --> S4["S = 30%+
mu plateau then drop
Severe soil failure"] S4 --> S5["S = 100%
Full spin
mu approx 0
Tractor stuck"] ``` ## 4.5 Force Vector Resolution at the Contact Patch ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SOIL["SOIL (Reactive Medium)"] SH["Shear stress along contact patch:
tau approx c + sigma tan(phi)
(Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope)"] end subgraph TYRE["TYRE TREAD (Active)"] APPL["Applies shear: pushes soil BACKWARD"] end SOIL -->|Reaction T_g| TYRE TYRE -->|Applies shear| SOIL ``` > [!NOTE] > **Board Diagram Note:** In KTU 14-mark answers, the free-body diagram **must** show $W$, $T_g$, $R$, and $DP$ with clearly marked directions. The torque $M$ at the axle (drawn as a curved arrow) earns an extra half-mark for completeness. Always label soil beneath the contact patch. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]** **Define the terms: (i) Gross traction, (ii) Net traction, and (iii) Coefficient of traction. State the relationship between them.** **Model Answer:** - **(i) Gross Traction ($T_g$):** The total horizontal force developed at the tyre–soil contact patch as a result of the driving torque applied at the wheel axle. It is the soil's reaction to the shearing action of the tyre tread. **Unit: N (Newton).** - **(ii) Net Traction ($T_n$):** The portion of gross traction that remains available for useful work (pulling an implement) after the losses due to motion resistance have been subtracted. **Unit: N.** - **(iii) Coefficient of Traction ($\mu$):** A dimensionless index defined as the ratio of net traction to the dynamic vertical load carried by the wheel. $$\mu = \frac{T_n}{W} = \frac{T_g - R}{W} = \mu_g - \rho$$ --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]** **What is wheel slip? Why is it considered a key parameter in traction mechanics?** **Model Answer:** Wheel slip is the fractional difference between the theoretical forward speed of a driven wheel (computed as $V_t = \omega \cdot r$) and its actual measured ground speed ($V_a$). It is expressed as: $$S = \frac{V_t - V_a}{V_t}$$ **Importance in traction mechanics:** 1. Slip is the **primary indicator of soil shear engagement** between tyre and soil. 2. The **coefficient of traction** $\mu$ is a function of slip ($\mu = f(S)$); no traction can be developed without some slip on soft soil. 3. **Excess slip** indicates energy waste as soil deformation and heat — reducing tractive efficiency and increasing fuel consumption. 4. It is the **diagnostic parameter** used in KTU lab testing and ASAE standard S296 to grade tractor performance. 5. Optimal operating slip is typically **10–15%** for 2WD tractors on firm soil. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]** **(a)** A 2WD agricultural tractor operates on a tilled field. Each drive wheel carries a dynamic vertical load of $W = 11{,}250$ N. During a drawbar pull test, the measured data are: - Driving torque per wheel: $M = 1{,}350$ N·m - Rolling radius: $r = 0.75$ m - Gross traction per wheel: $T_g = 8{,}400$ N - Motion resistance per wheel: $R = 3{,}150$ N - Theoretical forward speed: $V_t = 2.0$ m/s - Actual forward speed: $V_a = 1.7$ m/s Calculate the **net traction**, **coefficient of traction**, **motion resistance ratio**, **wheel slip (in %)**, **drawbar pull per wheel**, and the **tractive efficiency**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the aid of a labelled free-body diagram, explain the **origin of gross traction** at the tyre–soil contact patch. Discuss how the Mohr–Coulomb shear strength parameters of soil ($c$ and $\phi$) influence the maximum tractive force that can be developed. **(7 marks)** --- ### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A** #### **Part (a) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given:** $W = 11{,}250$ N, $T_g = 8{,}400$ N, $R = 3{,}150$ N, $V_t = 2.0$ m/s, $V_a = 1.7$ m/s. **Step 1 — Net traction:** $$T_n = T_g - R = 8{,}400 - 3{,}150 = 5{,}250 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2 — Coefficient of traction:** $$\mu = \frac{T_n}{W} = \frac{5{,}250}{11{,}250} = 0.467$$ **Step 3 — Motion resistance ratio:** $$\rho = \frac{R}{W} = \frac{3{,}150}{11{,}250} = 0.280$$ **Step 4 — Gross traction coefficient:** $$\mu_g = \frac{T_g}{W} = \frac{8{,}400}{11{,}250} = 0.747$$ **Verification:** $\mu = \mu_g - \rho = 0.747 - 0.280 = 0.467$ ✓ **Step 5 — Wheel slip:** $$S = \frac{V_t - V_a}{V_t} = \frac{2.0 - 1.7}{2.0} = 0.15 = 15\%$$ **Step 6 — Drawbar pull per wheel:** Under steady state, $DP = T_n = 5{,}250$ N. For both drive wheels: $$DP_{total} = 2 \times 5{,}250 = 10{,}500 \text{ N}$$ **Step 7 — Tractive efficiency:** $$\eta_t = \frac{DP \cdot V_a}{T_g \cdot V_t} = \frac{5{,}250 \times 1.7}{8{,}400 \times 2.0} = \frac{8{,}925}{16{,}800} = 0.5313 = 53.1\%$$ Or equivalently: $$\eta_t = \left(1 - \frac{\rho}{\mu_g}\right)(1 - S) = \left(1 - \frac{0.280}{0.747}\right)(0.85) = (0.625)(0.85) = 0.531 \quad \checkmark$$ #### **Part (b) — Theoretical Solution (7 marks)** **Free-body diagram (as drawn in Section 4.1):** A driven wheel on soil experiences: - $W$ acting downward at the axle. - $T_g$ acting forward at the contact patch (soil reaction). - $R$ acting backward (motion resistance). - $M$ (driving torque) acting clockwise at the axle. **Origin of Gross Traction:** When torque $M$ is applied at the axle, the tyre tread attempts to rotate relative to the soil. Due to friction-like adhesion and mechanical interlocking of the tread lugs with soil aggregates, a **shear stress** $\tau$ develops along the contact patch. The tyre exerts a **backward** shear on the soil. By Newton's Third Law, the soil exerts an **equal and opposite forward shear** on the tyre. The integral of this shear stress over the contact area is the **gross traction**: $$T_g = \int_A \tau \, dA$$ **Mohr–Coulomb Influence:** Soil shear strength is governed by the Mohr–Coulomb failure criterion: $$\tau_{max} = c + \sigma \tan\phi$$ where: - $c$ = **cohesion** of soil (N/m²) - $\sigma$ = **normal stress** on the shear plane (N/m²) - $\phi$ = **angle of internal friction** (degrees) **Effect on $T_g$:** 1. **Cohesive soils (clay, wet loam):** High $c$, low $\phi$. Gross traction is dominated by the cohesive component. Such soils give good traction at low slip but lose strength when wet-shear failure occurs. 2. **Cohesionless soils (dry sand):** Low $c$, high $\phi$. Gross traction depends strongly on the normal stress (wheel load $W$) and $\tan\phi$. Best traction is achieved by increasing contact area and ballast. 3. **Increase in $W$** increases $\sigma$, which increases $\tau_{max}$ linearly for frictional soils, and hence increases $T_g$ (up to a limit set by soil bearing failure). 4. **Excessive $W$** causes **sinkage** and rutting, which raises $R$ faster than it raises $T_g$, thereby **reducing** $T_n$. The maximum possible gross traction before complete soil failure (i.e., the wheel spinning in place) is reached when the soil beneath the contact patch is sheared along its full extent. Beyond this limit, increasing torque or load no longer increases $T_g$ — it only wastes energy as soil churning. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]** **(a)** Define **wheel slip** and **tractive efficiency**. Derive the expression: $$\eta_t = \left(1 - \frac{\rho}{\mu_g}\right)(1 - S)$$ starting from the basic force balance of a driven wheel. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A tractor develops a gross traction of $T_g = 12{,}000$ N on a soft paddy field. The motion resistance is $R = 4{,}500$ N, and the wheel slip is $S = 0.20$. Determine: (i) the net traction, (ii) the tractive efficiency, (iii) the actual forward speed if the theoretical speed is $V_t = 2.4$ m/s, and (iv) the drawbar power output, assuming steady state. **(7 marks)** --- ### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B** #### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)** **Definitions:** - **Wheel Slip ($S$):** The fractional reduction in actual forward speed of a driven wheel relative to its theoretical speed (computed from rolling radius and angular velocity): $$S = \frac{V_t - V_a}{V_t}$$ - **Tractive Efficiency ($\eta_t$):** The dimensionless ratio of the useful drawbar power output to the gross traction power input at the wheel–soil interface: $$\eta_t = \frac{P_{drawbar}}{P_{gross}} = \frac{DP \cdot V_a}{T_g \cdot V_t}$$ **Derivation:** **Step 1 — Steady-state horizontal force balance:** At constant velocity, with $DP$ as the external drawbar pull: $$T_g - R - DP = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad DP = T_g - R$$ **Step 2 — Express net traction:** $$T_n = T_g - R = DP$$ **Step 3 — Express actual speed using slip:** $$V_a = V_t(1 - S)$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into tractive efficiency definition:** $$\eta_t = \frac{(T_g - R) \cdot V_t(1 - S)}{T_g \cdot V_t}$$ **Step 5 — Cancel $V_t$:** $$\eta_t = \frac{T_g - R}{T_g} \cdot (1 - S) = \left(1 - \frac{R}{T_g}\right)(1 - S)$$ **Step 6 — Convert resistance ratio:** Since $\rho = R/W$ and $\mu_g = T_g/W$, we have $R/T_g = \rho/\mu_g$. Substituting: $$\boxed{\eta_t = \left(1 - \frac{\rho}{\mu_g}\right)(1 - S)}$$ **Interpretation:** Tractive efficiency drops with both **higher motion resistance ratio** (soft, deformable, or muddy soil) and **higher wheel slip** (excess soil deformation, poor traction). #### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given:** $T_g = 12{,}000$ N, $R = 4{,}500$ N, $S = 0.20$, $V_t = 2.4$ m/s. **(i) Net traction:** $$T_n = T_g - R = 12{,}000 - 4{,}500 = 7{,}500 \text{ N}$$ **(ii) Tractive efficiency:** $$\eta_t = \left(1 - \frac{R}{T_g}\right)(1 - S) = \left(1 - \frac{4{,}500}{12{,}000}\right)(1 - 0.20)$$ $$\eta_t = (1 - 0.375)(0.80) = 0.625 \times 0.80 = 0.500 = 50.0\%$$ **(iii) Actual forward speed:** $$V_a = V_t(1 - S) = 2.4 \times (1 - 0.20) = 2.4 \times 0.80 = 1.92 \text{ m/s}$$ **(iv) Drawbar power output:** Under steady state, $DP = T_n = 7{,}500$ N. $$P_{drawbar} = DP \cdot V_a = 7{,}500 \times 1.92 = 14{,}400 \text{ W} = 14.4 \text{ kW}$$ **Cross-verification using tractive efficiency:** $$P_{gross} = T_g \cdot V_t = 12{,}000 \times 2.4 = 28{,}800 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{drawbar} = \eta_t \cdot P_{gross} = 0.500 \times 28{,}800 = 14{,}400 \text{ W} \quad \checkmark$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Module 4:** > > 1. **Confusing Gross and Net Traction:** Many students write $DP = T_g$. This is wrong. $DP = T_n = T_g - R$. Losing 1–2 marks per occurrence. > > 2. **Forgetting the units in the slip formula:** Slip is **dimensionless** when expressed as a decimal, but must be converted to a fraction (0.16) — not 16 — when substituting into $\eta_t = (1 - \rho/\mu_g)(1 - S)$. Using $S = 16$ will destroy the answer. > > 3. **Omitting the free-body diagram in long answers:** A 7-mark sub-question in Part B explicitly asks for a diagram. Drawing it earns 1–2 marks even if the rest is partially wrong. > > 4. **Mixing $V_t$ and $V_a$ in the tractive efficiency formula:** $V_a$ belongs in the numerator, $V_t$ in the denominator. Reverse them and you get a fictitious efficiency > 1. > > 5. **Treating traction as pure friction:** The single biggest conceptual error. Always link $T_g$ to **soil shear strength** (Mohr–Coulomb) in any descriptive answer. > > 6. **Neglecting contact pressure:** When asked about improving traction, ballast ($W$ increase) and reduced inflation pressure (larger $A$, lower $P$) are standard answers. Stating only "add weight" without addressing inflation pressure loses a mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Introduction to Traction and Mechanics** > > - **Traction** = sustained frictional + shear interaction at tyre–soil contact that produces horizontal propulsive force. > - **Four cardinal forces on a driven wheel:** $W$ (down), $T_g$ (forward), $R$ (backward), $DP$ (backward at hitch). > - **Master Equation #1:** $T_n = T_g - R$ (net traction balance). > - **Master Equation #2:** $\mu = \mu_g - \rho$ (coefficient decomposition). > - **Master Equation #3:** $S = (V_t - V_a)/V_t$ (slip definition). > - **Master Equation #4:** $\eta_t = (1 - \rho/\mu_g)(1 - S)$ (tractive efficiency). > - Under **steady-state rolling**, $DP = T_n$ (numerical equality of drawbar pull and net traction). > - **Optimal slip** = 10–15% (2WD, firm soil); 20–30% (loose sand or tracks). > - **Soil shear strength** is governed by $\tau = c + \sigma\tan\phi$ (Mohr–Coulomb); both $c$ and $\phi$ contribute to $T_g$. > - **Tractive efficiency** decreases as either **slip** or **motion resistance ratio** increases. > - **Increasing wheel load $W$** raises $T_g$ (good for frictional soils) but also raises $R$ (bad for soft soil) — net effect is non-linear. > - **Lowering inflation pressure** enlarges contact patch area $A$, reduces mean contact pressure $P = W/A$, and improves traction on soft soil. > - For exam: **always draw the free-body diagram**, **always state units**, and **always show the verification step** in numerical answers. > - Peak tractive efficiency and peak coefficient of traction occur at **different slip values** — a classic viva question.

topics.title: Off Road Traction and Mobility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT743 - MECHANICS OF TILLAGE AND TRACTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Off Road Traction and Mobility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Off-road traction** is the engineering science that quantifies the ability of a vehicle's running gear (wheels or tracks) to generate a **propulsive (tractive) force** through soil–wheel interaction, while simultaneously managing the **resistive forces** (compaction, shearing, and bulldozing of soil) that oppose forward motion. The study of off-road traction and mobility is therefore concerned with predicting the **drawbar pull**, **rolling resistance**, **wheel slip**, and **tractive efficiency** of vehicles operating on unprepared, deformable, and often heterogeneous terrain such as agricultural fields, forest floors, deserts, snow, and mud.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mobility (KTU Definition):** Mobility is defined as the *capacity of a vehicle to traverse a given terrain and to sustain a useful drawbar pull while doing so*. The dimensionless **Mobility Number** $M_n$, developed from dimensional analysis, is the single most important KTU-board index used to compare the intrinsic "go-anywhere" capability of one vehicle against another on a given soil.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pedestrian on Wet Sand" Model

Imagine you are walking briskly across a hard, dry concrete floor. You place your foot down, push backward, and the floor reacts with an equal-and-opposite forward force on your shoe. Your shoe **does not slip** because the floor does not deform — almost 100 % of your effort is converted into forward motion. That is **ideal traction**.

Now imagine the *same* brisk walk on a wet, sandy beach. The moment you push backward, the sand granules flow around your foot, the foot sinks in, and a noticeable fraction of your muscular energy is "lost" into churning the sand instead of propelling you forward. Some sand is bulldozed ahead, and a permanent footprint is left behind. To maintain speed, you must lift your foot earlier — meaning your foot **slips** relative to the sand.

The *pedestrian-on-sand* example maps directly to a pneumatic tractor tyre on a tilled agricultural field:

| Pedestrian Component | Off-Road Vehicle Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Foot placement on sand | Tyre contact patch on soft soil |
| Foot sliding back | **Wheel slip (s)** |
| Effort wasted deforming sand | **Motion resistance / rolling resistance ($R_r$)** |
| Net forward push you feel | **Net traction / Drawbar pull ($DP$)** |
| Footprint depth | **Rut depth / sinkage (z)** |

The harder (stiffer) the soil and the larger and softer the contact patch, the closer the system behaves to the concrete-floor case — and the higher the tractive efficiency.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally used in KTU 2024 Scheme problems:

- **Cone Index (CI):** expressed in **kPa** (or psi in older US literature). Typical agricultural soil: 300 – 1500 kPa.
- **Wheel Slip (s):** dimensionless ratio, **0 ≤ s ≤ 1** (0.2 is a common design target for tractors).
- **Coefficient of Traction (μ):** dimensionless, 0.3 – 0.8 typical.
- **Coefficient of Rolling Resistance (ρ):** dimensionless, 0.05 – 0.20 for agricultural tyres.
- **Gross Vehicle Weight (W):** expressed in **N** (or kN).
- **Tyre Section Height (h) and Section Width (b):** in **metres**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU 2024:** Module 4 questions frequently ask students to compute **net traction**, **drawbar pull**, and **tractive efficiency** given the soil strength parameters (CI, $k_c$, $k_\phi$, $c$, $\phi$) and wheel dimensions. Memorise the three dimensionless groups — **Mobility Number $M_n$, Motion Resistance Number $M_r$, and Traction Number $M_t$** — they are the gateway to every numerical in this module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Coupled Soil–Wheel Phenomena

Off-road traction emerges from the *simultaneous* action of three physical phenomena occurring at the tyre–soil interface. Mastering the distinction among them is the single highest-leverage revision step for KTU 2024.

1. **Vertical Compaction (Sinkage) — Bekker's Pressure-Sinkage Law**
   - As the wheel loads the soil, the contact patch *sinks* into the surface. The vertical stress $p$ required to produce a sinkage $z$ follows the empirical Bekker equation.
   - **Why it matters:** Sinkage directly increases motion resistance and dictates the choice of tyre inflation pressure and contact patch geometry.

2. **Tangential Shear (Slip Shear) — Janosi–Hanamoto Equation**
   - As the wheel rotates, every point on the contact patch attempts to move rearward relative to the soil. The soil resists this with a shear stress $\tau$ that builds with slip displacement $j$ until it saturates at the soil's shear strength.
   - **Why it matters:** The integrated tangential shear stress is the only mechanism generating a horizontal propulsive force on the vehicle. Without shear resistance, the wheel would spin in place.

3. **Passive Failure (Bulldozing)**
   - The leading edge of the tyre pushes soil forward, creating a bow wave of displaced material. This consumes energy and contributes to motion resistance.
   - **Why it matters:** At high sinkage (soft soil, high loads), bulldozing becomes the *dominant* resistive term.

## 2.2 Bekker's Pressure–Sinkage Equation

Empirically fitted to plate-sinkage tests, the vertical pressure $p$ beneath a loaded area is:

$$p = \left(\frac{k_c}{b} + k_\phi \right) z^{n}$$

where:
- $k_c$ = **cohesive modulus of soil deformation** (kN/m$^{n+1}$)
- $k_\phi$ = **frictional modulus of soil deformation** (kN/m$^{n+2}$)
- $b$ = smaller dimension (width) of the loaded plate (m)
- $n$ = **sinkage exponent** (dimensionless, typically 0.5 – 1.0; $n = 1$ for agricultural soil, $n = 0.5$ for sand)
- $z$ = sinkage (m)

The total vertical load on a plate of area $A$ is recovered by integrating this pressure over the area:

$$W = \int_A p \, dA = b \int_0^{z_{\max}} p(z)\, dz \quad \Rightarrow \quad W = b \, z_{\max}^{\,n+1} \left( k_c + b\, k_\phi \right)$$

## 2.3 Janosi–Hanamoto Shear–Displacement Equation

The shear stress $\tau$ at slip displacement $j$ is:

$$\tau(j) = \tau_{\max} \left( 1 - e^{-j / K} \right)$$

where:
- $\tau_{\max} = (c + p \tan\phi)$ = Mohr–Coulomb shear strength at the local normal pressure $p$
- $c$ = **soil cohesion** (kPa)
- $\phi$ = **soil internal friction angle** (degrees)
- $K$ = **shear deformation modulus** (m), typically 0.01 – 0.05 m for agricultural soils

## 2.4 Net Traction and Drawbar Pull

The total horizontal force the wheel can transmit is the **gross traction** $H$, obtained by integrating the shear stress over the contact patch. Part of this is consumed overcoming **motion resistance** $R$. The remainder is the **drawbar pull** $DP$:

$$DP = H - R$$

The **Coefficient of Net Traction (CNT)** is then:

$$CNT = \frac{DP}{W}$$

## 2.5 Wheel Slip (s)

Wheel slip is the fractional loss of forward travel due to wheel rotation exceeding the theoretical no-slip travel:

$$s = 1 - \frac{V}{r\,\omega} = 1 - \frac{V_a}{V_t}$$

where:
- $V$ = **actual forward velocity of the vehicle** (m/s)
- $r$ = **wheel rolling radius** (m)
- $\omega$ = **angular velocity of the wheel** (rad/s)
- $V_t = r\omega$ = theoretical (no-slip) velocity

## 2.6 Tractive Efficiency (TE)

Tractive efficiency is the ratio of useful drawbar power to input axle power:

$$TE = \frac{DP \cdot V}{H \cdot V_t} = \frac{DP}{H} \cdot (1 - s)$$

## 2.7 The Wong Mobility / Resistance / Traction Numbers

Through dimensional analysis of the soil–wheel system, **J.Y. Wong** (the canonical KTU reference) showed that off-road performance collapses onto three dimensionless groups.

### 2.7.1 Mobility Number $M_n$

$$M_n = \frac{CI \cdot b \cdot d}{W} \cdot \left( \frac{\delta}{h} \right)^{1/2}$$

where:
- $CI$ = **Wescone cone index** (kPa)
- $b, d$ = tyre width and diameter (m)
- $W$ = dynamic wheel load (N)
- $\delta / h$ = tyre deflection-to-section-height ratio (≈ 0.20 for agricultural tyres, 0.33 for military)

A **lower** $M_n$ means the vehicle is *more* mobile on the given soil.

### 2.7.2 Motion Resistance Number $M_r$

$$M_r = \frac{R}{W} = f\left(M_n, \text{ tyre type} \right)$$

Read from Wong's universal charts.

### 2.7.3 Traction Number $M_t$

$$M_t = \frac{H}{W} = f\left(M_n, s, \text{ tyre type} \right)$$

Also read from Wong's charts.

## 2.8 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **only** set of formulas you should memorise for the Module 4 board exam. Every numerical in KTU papers for this module reduces to one of these rows.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Key Inputs | Typical Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bekker vertical pressure | $p = \left(\dfrac{k_c}{b} + k_\phi\right) z^{n}$ | $k_c, k_\phi, b, n, z$ | kPa |
| 2 | Plate sinkage load | $W = b\, z_{\max}^{\,n+1} (k_c + b\,k_\phi)$ | $b, z_{\max}, k_c, k_\phi, n$ | N |
| 3 | Janosi shear stress | $\tau = \tau_{\max}\!\left(1 - e^{-j/K}\right)$ | $c, \phi, p, j, K$ | kPa |
| 4 | Mohr–Coulomb shear | $\tau_{\max} = c + p \tan\phi$ | $c, \phi, p$ | kPa |
| 5 | Wheel slip | $s = 1 - \dfrac{V}{r\omega}$ | $V, r, \omega$ | 0 – 1 |
| 6 | Drawbar pull | $DP = H - R$ | $H, R$ | N |
| 7 | Coefficient of net traction | $CNT = DP / W$ | $DP, W$ | 0.2 – 0.7 |
| 8 | Tractive efficiency | $TE = (DP / H)(1 - s)$ | $DP, H, s$ | 0 – 1 |
| 9 | Mobility Number | $M_n = \dfrac{CI \cdot b \cdot d}{W} \left(\dfrac{\delta}{h}\right)^{1/2}$ | $CI, b, d, W, \delta, h$ | 0.1 – 1000 |
| 10 | Motion Resistance | $M_r = R / W$ | from Wong chart | 0.03 – 0.30 |
| 11 | Traction Number | $M_t = H / W$ | from Wong chart | 0.1 – 0.9 |
| 12 | Compaction resistance | $R_c = \dfrac{b\, z_{\max}^{\,n+1}}{(n+1)} \left( k_c + b\,k_\phi \right)$ | $b, z_{\max}, k_c, k_\phi, n$ | N |
| 13 | Bekker bulldozing resistance | $R_b = b \left( z_{\max} \cdot c + \dfrac{1}{2} z_{\max}^{2} \rho_s g \, K_p \right)$ | $b, c, \rho_s, g, K_p$ | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU 2024:** For *rigid* wheels on *agricultural* soil, $n = 1.0$; for *flexible* (pneumatic) tyres, $n = 0.5$ — 1.0. For *sand*, $n = 0.5$ regardless of wheel type. These values are usually *given* in the problem statement, but the examiner may test whether you correctly pair the right $n$ with the right soil–wheel combination.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Agricultural Tractor Design:** Manufacturers use Wong's charts to specify the smallest possible tractor that can still pull a 6-bottom plough through a wet Vertisol without exceeding 15 % slip.
2. **Military Vehicle Procurement:** The US Army's **NRMM (NASA Reference Mobility Model)** is a direct descendant of Wong's theory and is used to certify vehicles such as the Bradley and the Stryker for off-road operations.
3. **Forestry and Mining Equipment:** Skidders and haul-trucks operate on the softest soils on the planet. Predictive mobility models reduce the need for expensive and disruptive road-building.
4. **Planetary Rovers (NASA):** The Mars rover *Curiosity* was mobility-modelled using the *Bekker–Wong* framework to predict wheel sinkage and traction on regolith.
5. **Autonomous Off-Road Robotics:** Modern agricultural robots (e.g., for weeding and spraying) require predictive traction control — exactly the closed-form expressions taught in this module.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bekker's Pressure-Sinkage Equation from Plate Bearing Tests

Bekker (1956) performed numerous plate-bearing tests on various soils with circular and rectangular plates of varying widths. He proposed that the *pressure–sinkage* relation of a soil under a flat plate could be represented as:

$$p = k z^{n}$$

where $k$ is a soil-specific modulus. However, experimental data showed that $k$ was *not* invariant — it depended strongly on the plate width $b$. Bekker therefore decomposed $k$ into a cohesive part and a frictional part:

$$k = \frac{k_c}{b} + k_\phi$$

This decomposition has a clear physical basis: the wider the plate, the deeper its stress bulb extends into the soil, engaging more frictional grains. Substituting back:

$$p = \left(\frac{k_c}{b} + k_\phi \right) z^{n}$$

Multiplying both sides by the plate area $A$ and integrating from $0$ to $z_{\max}$ gives the total plate load:

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{0}^{z_{\max}} p(z) \cdot b \, dz \\
&= b \int_{0}^{z_{\max}} \left(\frac{k_c}{b} + k_\phi \right) z^{n} dz \\
&= b \left(\frac{k_c}{b} + k_\phi \right) \int_{0}^{z_{\max}} z^{n} dz \\
&= b \left(\frac{k_c}{b} + k_\phi \right) \left[ \frac{z^{n+1}}{n+1} \right]_{0}^{z_{\max}} \\
&= \frac{b \, z_{\max}^{\,n+1}}{n+1} \left( k_c + b \, k_\phi \right)
\end{aligned}$$

Solving for the maximum sinkage produced by a given load $W$:

$$z_{\max} = \left[ \frac{(n+1)\, W}{b (k_c + b\,k_\phi)} \right]^{1/(n+1)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This expression is essential for KTU 2024 numerical problems where you are given $W$ and asked to find the rut depth under a wheel, or vice versa.

## 3.2 Derivation: Motion Resistance of a Rigid Wheel on Deformable Soil

The motion resistance $R$ of a wheel is the *horizontal* force the soil exerts on the wheel as the wheel moves forward. For a rigid wheel rolling on a deformable surface, $R$ has two components:

**1. Compaction resistance $R_c$** — energy spent in compacting soil behind the wheel:

$$R_c = b \int_{0}^{z_{\max}} p(z) \, dz = \frac{b\, z_{\max}^{\,n+1}}{n+1} \left( k_c + b\, k_\phi \right) = \frac{W}{n+1}$$

**2. Bulldozing resistance $R_b$** — passive earth-pressure failure in front of the leading edge of the wheel. Bekker approximated this using Rankine passive earth pressure:

$$R_b = b \left( c\, z_{\max} + \frac{1}{2} \rho_s\, g\, z_{\max}^{2} K_p \right)$$

where:
- $\rho_s$ = **soil bulk density** (kg/m³)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)
- $K_p$ = **Rankine passive earth-pressure coefficient** = $\tan^{2}\!\left(45^\circ + \phi/2\right)$

Summing both contributions, the total motion resistance of a rigid wheel is:

$$R = R_c + R_b = \frac{W}{n+1} + b \left( c\, z_{\max} + \frac{1}{2} \rho_s\, g\, z_{\max}^{2} K_p \right)$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Gross Traction Force (H) for a Rigid Wheel

The gross traction $H$ is the integral of the tangential shear stress $\tau$ over the contact area. Consider a small element of the contact patch of width $b$ and arc-length $dl = r\, d\theta$ on the wheel.

The slip displacement at a contact point depends on its angular position. For a wheel with slip $s$ and forward velocity $V$, the slip velocity at the contact patch is $V_t - V = V \cdot s / (1 - s)$. For an element entering the contact patch at angle $\theta$ from the leading edge, the slip displacement is:

$$j(\theta) = r\theta \, \frac{s}{1 - s}$$

The Janosi–Hanamoto shear at this element is:

$$\tau(\theta) = (c + p \tan\phi) \left( 1 - e^{-r\theta s / \left[ K(1-s) \right] } \right)$$

The gross traction is the integral of $\tau$ over the contact patch length $L_c \approx \sqrt{2 r z_{\max}}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= b \int_{0}^{\theta_m} \tau(\theta) \, r \, d\theta \\
&= b r (c + p \tan\phi) \int_{0}^{\theta_m} \left( 1 - e^{-A \theta} \right) d\theta \quad \text{where } A = \dfrac{rs}{K(1-s)} \\
&= b r (c + p \tan\phi) \left[ \theta_m + \frac{e^{-A\theta_m} - 1}{A} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

In KTU 2024 problems, the examiner typically provides the *peak* tractive force per unit contact area, in which case the gross traction reduces to:

$$H = b L_c (c + p \tan\phi) \left( 1 + \frac{K(1-s)}{r s}\right) - \frac{b r (c + p \tan\phi) K(1-s)}{r s}$$

A widely-used engineering simplification (the **"fully-mobilised shear"** assumption, where the slip is large enough that $1 - e^{-A\theta_m} \approx 1$) gives the elegant closed form used in most KTU numericals:

$$H = b L_c (c + W \tan\phi / A_c)$$

where $A_c = b L_c$ is the contact patch area.

## 3.4 Derivation: Wong's Mobility Number from Dimensional Analysis

Consider the wheel–soil system. The relevant physical variables are:

| Variable | Symbol | Dimension |
|---|---|---|
| Wheel diameter | $d$ | L |
| Wheel width | $b$ | L |
| Wheel load | $W$ | F |
| Tyre deflection ratio | $\delta/h$ | — |
| Soil cone index | $CI$ | F L$^{-2}$ |

By the **Buckingham $\Pi$ theorem**, the three fundamental dimensions F, L, T collapse to $5 - 3 = 2$ dimensionless groups. The two canonical groups chosen by Wong are:

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{CI \cdot b \cdot d}{W}, \qquad \Pi_2 = \frac{\delta}{h}$$

Wong combined these into a single composite **Mobility Number**:

$$M_n = \Pi_1 \cdot \sqrt{\Pi_2} = \frac{CI \cdot b \cdot d}{W} \sqrt{\frac{\delta}{h}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The $\sqrt{\delta/h}$ factor** accounts for the fact that a *flexible* (deflected) tyre conforms to the soil surface, increasing contact patch area and distributing load more evenly. For a rigid wheel ($\delta = 0$), $M_n \to 0$ and the system behaviour is governed by a different (more bulldozing-dominated) regime.

## 3.5 Worked Analytical Matrix — Predicting Vehicle Performance

The following analytical matrix is the **canonical workflow** expected in KTU 2024 numerical answers:

| Step | Question to Answer | Required Inputs | Tool / Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | What is the soil strength? | $c, \phi, CI, k_c, k_\phi, n$ | Field tests (cone penetrometer, Bevameter) |
| 2 | What is the rut depth $z_{\max}$ under load? | $W, b, k_c, k_\phi, n$ | Bekker sinkage equation |
| 3 | What is the motion resistance $R$? | $z_{\max}, c, \phi, \rho_s, b$ | $R = R_c + R_b$ |
| 4 | What is the gross traction $H$ at slip $s$? | $s, W, b, L_c, c, \phi, K$ | Janosi–Hanamoto integral |
| 5 | What is the drawbar pull $DP$? | $H, R$ | $DP = H - R$ |
| 6 | What is the tractive efficiency $TE$? | $DP, H, s$ | $TE = (DP/H)(1-s)$ |
| 7 | Is the design acceptable? | Compare $s$ and $TE$ with targets | $s \le 0.20$, $TE \ge 0.70$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

```mermaid
    S1 --> A3

    S2 --> S3[Sinkage z_max]
    S3 --> R1[Compaction Resistance Rc]

    R2 --> R3

    O2 --> O3

```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Wong Mobility Prediction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Field Soil Tests\nCone Penetrometer\nBevameter] --> DB[(Soil Parameter Database\nCI, c, phi, kc, kphi, n, K)]
    IN2[Wheel Specification\nd, b, delta, h, r] --> DB
    DB --> CALC1[Step 1: Compute Mobility Number Mn]
    CALC1 --> CHART1[Wong Universal Chart\nMotion Resistance Mr]
    CHART1 --> RVAL[Motion Resistance R = Mr x W]
    DB --> CALC2[Step 2: Compute Gross Traction H]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Free-Body Diagram of a Powered Wheel (Textual)

```
                       ↑ N  (Vertical load on wheel)
                       │
                ┌──────┴──────┐
                │   ●─────→ V │  ← Wheel centre
                │   │         │     (V = forward velocity)
                │   │ W       │     (W = dynamic load)
                └────┬────────┘
                       │
                       ↓
        ┌───────────── Soil ─────────────┐
        │                                │
        │     ◀── j  (slip displacement) │
        │   ────────────────────────     │
        │     τ = (c + p tanφ) (1-e^(-j/K))
        │                                │
        │  Leading edge   Trailing edge  │
        │  (bulldozing)   (compacted)    │
        └────────────────────────────────┘
        ◀── R (motion resistance)    H ──▶
                                    (gross traction)
        Net Drawbar Pull DP = H − R  ──▶
```
